criteria.title: Demonstrate Why the Organisation Is the Right Partner
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to understand why this organisation adds value to the consortium.
criteria.example_strong:
"This organisation is a non-profit entity working in environmental education, youth work and community-based learning, with a strong focus on sustainability, social inclusion and non-formal education methodologies.

It has proven experience in Erasmus+ project implementation, including the development of training materials, educational tools and participatory methodologies tested directly with young people, schools and local communities. For example, it has designed and implemented workshops on permaculture-based learning, emotional development activities for youth at risk of social exclusion, and school-based environmental education programmes combining hands-on gardening, teamwork and digital learning tools.

Its expertise in non-formal education design, field implementation and stakeholder engagement contributes directly to the quality, practical relevance and European impact of the project results.
."

Avoid copying generic text from the organisation's website without connecting it to the project.

*Would you like to make any comments or add any information to the summary of your organisation’s past participation? 3000

general_context:
This question provides an opportunity to reinforce the organisation's added value beyond the automatic Erasmus+ participation summary.
The evaluator already has access to the organisation's participation history through the Erasmus+ database.
Therefore, they are not asking for a repetition of that information.
Instead, they want to understand:
"Why is this previous experience important for the proposed project?"
This is your opportunity to explain how the organisation's accumulated experience has strengthened its expertise, international networks, management capacity and ability to deliver high-quality European projects.
Rather than repeating statistics or project titles, explain how the organisation has evolved through its previous participation and why this makes it a valuable partner for the current proposal.
writing_guidance:
This question comes after the organisational profile and experience.
It acts as a final opportunity to reinforce credibility and demonstrate organisational maturity.

general_context: This question belongs to the Relevance award criterion and is one of the key places where evaluators assess whether the project is genuinely aligned with Erasmus+ priorities. The purpose is not to demonstrate knowledge of the priorities themselves, but to show that the project has been deliberately designed to contribute to the priorities selected in the application form. Evaluators are looking for evidence that the priorities are embedded in the project's logic, objectives, activities, outputs, and expected impact. A strong answer makes it clear that the priorities are a core driver of the project rather than an administrative requirement completed during application preparation.
writing_guidance: This question appears after applicants have already described the needs, challenges, target groups, and objectives of the project. As a result, it should not repeat the problem analysis or provide a detailed description of every activity. Instead, it serves as a bridge between the project's rationale and the Erasmus+ policy framework. The evaluator wants to understand why the selected priorities are relevant to the identified needs and how the project's proposed solutions will contribute to addressing them. In practice, this means explaining how the challenges identified in the needs analysis connect to the priorities and how the project's activities and results will generate a meaningful contribution to those priorities.
connects_to: This question is closely connected to several other sections of the application. It is supported by the needs analysis, because the identified needs should justify the choice of priorities. It is also supported by the project objectives, which should reflect the changes that the selected priorities seek to promote. At the same time, this section feeds into the description of activities, expected results, impact, and sustainability. The priorities should be visible throughout the entire intervention logic of the project. Evaluators should be able to follow a clear chain from the identified challenge, to the selected priority, to the planned activities, and finally to the expected outcomes and long-term impact. A strong answer therefore demonstrates coherence and consistency across the whole proposal.

criteria.title: Direct Alignment with the Selected Priority
criteria.intent: The primary objective of this criterion is to demonstrate that the selected priority is genuinely relevant to the project and not simply included because it appears attractive or popular. Evaluators need to be convinced that there is a natural and logical relationship between the project's challenges and the chosen priority.
criteria.elements: The answer should explain what challenge or need relates to the priority, why the priority is relevant for the target groups, and how the project has been designed to respond to that challenge. Each selected priority should have its own clear explanation.
criteria.example_weak: "Our project addresses digital transformation because we will create an online platform."
criteria.example_strong: "The project addresses the Digital Transformation priority by responding to identified gaps in the digital competences of adult learners. Through the development of digital learning resources, trainer capacity-building activities, and the implementation of online learning methodologies, the project will strengthen participants' ability to use digital tools effectively."
criteria.avoid: Avoid copying text directly from the Erasmus+ Programme Guide, listing priorities without explanation, or assuming that mentioning a priority automatically demonstrates alignment.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Through Activities
criteria.intent: Evaluators want to see how the priority is translated into concrete actions. This criterion focuses on demonstrating that the project's activities are practical mechanisms for addressing the selected priority.
criteria.elements: The answer should refer to specific activities such as training programmes, workshops, pilot actions, mentoring schemes, tool development, awareness campaigns, or capacity-building measures. The connection between the activity and the priority should be explicit.
criteria.example_weak: "The project promotes inclusion through its activities."
criteria.example_strong: "To address the Inclusion and Diversity priority, the project will develop accessible learning materials, organise targeted outreach actions for underrepresented groups, and pilot adapted learning pathways designed to reduce participation barriers."
criteria.avoid: Avoid vague statements about activities or descriptions that fail to explain how the activity contributes to the selected priority.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Results and Contribution
criteria.intent: This criterion assesses whether the project is expected to produce meaningful outcomes that contribute to the selected priority. Evaluators are interested in the change created by the project, not only in the activities delivered.
criteria.elements: The answer should describe the expected outputs, the competences developed, the organisational improvements achieved, or the behavioural changes generated. The contribution to the priority should be visible in the expected results.
criteria.example_strong: "As a result of the training programme, participating organisations will strengthen their capacity to implement inclusive learning practices, while learners will gain new competences that improve their participation and engagement."
criteria.avoid: Avoid generic claims about benefits, unsupported promises, or results that cannot be clearly linked to the selected priority.

criteria.title: Demonstrate European Added Value
criteria.intent: The purpose of this criterion is to explain why the selected priority benefits from transnational cooperation and why the challenge cannot be addressed as effectively through a purely local initiative.
criteria.elements: The answer should highlight the exchange of good practices, mutual learning between partners, co-creation of solutions, testing in different contexts, and the transferability of results across countries.
criteria.example_weak: "Partners from different countries will work together."
criteria.example_strong: "By bringing together organisations from four countries facing similar challenges, the partnership will combine diverse expertise and test solutions in different environments, resulting in approaches that can be transferred and replicated across Europe."
criteria.avoid: Avoid describing partners individually without explaining how cooperation strengthens the project's contribution to the priority.

criteria.title: Evidence-Based Justification
criteria.intent: Evaluators are more convinced when priorities are supported by evidence rather than assumptions. This criterion demonstrates that the selected priorities respond to real and documented needs.
criteria.elements: The answer may refer to surveys, consultations, research findings, policy documents, previous project experience, stakeholder feedback, or statistical evidence that justifies the relevance of the priority.
criteria.example_strong: "A needs assessment conducted among participating organisations identified significant gaps in digital competences among staff and learners, confirming the relevance of the Digital Transformation priority and the need for targeted intervention."
criteria.avoid:
Avoid unsupported statements, broad generalisations, or claims that cannot be connected to evidence presented elsewhere in the proposal.

Please select up to three topics addressed by your project:

Project description
Please describe the motivation for your project and explain why it should be funded. (3000)

general_context:
This question belongs to the Relevance award criterion and is one of the most important sections of the entire application. Its purpose is to convince evaluators that there is a genuine need for the project and that investing Erasmus+ funding in the proposed solution is justified. Unlike questions that focus on objectives, activities, or results, this section focuses on the project's rationale. In simple terms, applicants must answer two fundamental questions: Why is this project needed? and Why should Erasmus+ fund it?
A strong answer demonstrates that the project responds to a clearly identified challenge, opportunity, or unmet need affecting the target groups. It should show that the proposed intervention is relevant, timely, and capable of generating meaningful benefits. Evaluators are not looking for a description of activities; they are looking for a convincing argument that explains why the project deserves support and why the proposed solution is worth investing in.
writing_guidance:
This question is usually one of the first opportunities to present the project's overall rationale. It establishes the foundation for everything that follows in the application. The needs, challenges, and opportunities described here should later be reflected in the objectives, activities, expected results, impact, and sustainability sections. If the motivation is weak, the rest of the proposal may appear unnecessary or disconnected.
A strong answer should be evidence-based rather than opinion-based. Whenever applicants claim that a problem exists, a gap needs to be addressed, or a target group requires support, these statements should ideally be supported by evidence. Statistics, research findings, surveys, stakeholder consultations, policy documents, previous project experience, sector studies, and other reliable sources help demonstrate that the identified need is real rather than assumed. Evaluators are generally more convinced by arguments supported by evidence than by broad statements that cannot be verified.
At the same time, this section is also an opportunity to engage the evaluator. Since evaluators review many applications, an answer that begins with a powerful and authentic quote, testimony, real-life situation, or striking fact can help create an immediate connection with the issue being addressed. Such an opening can make the challenge feel more tangible and human. However, emotional appeal should never replace evidence. The strongest applications combine a compelling introduction with reliable data and documented needs, allowing evaluators both to understand the issue and to trust that it is supported by facts.
The answer should ultimately explain not only what the challenge is, but also why the proposed project is the appropriate response and why Erasmus+ funding is justified.
connects_to:
This question supports many other sections of the application because it provides the justification for the entire project. The needs and challenges described here should explain why the objectives were chosen, why the selected priorities are relevant, and why the proposed activities are necessary. In this sense, the motivation section serves as the foundation upon which the intervention logic of the project is built.
At the same time, it feeds directly into later sections of the proposal. The expected results should respond to the needs identified here. The impact should demonstrate how the situation will improve as a result of the project. The sustainability strategy should explain how the benefits generated will continue beyond the funding period. A strong answer therefore creates a clear and logical thread that runs throughout the entire application, helping evaluators understand not only what the project will do, but why it matters.

criteria.title: Demonstrate a Genuine Need
criteria.intent: The evaluator must be convinced that the project responds to a real and significant need rather than an idea that the partnership simply finds interesting. The project should emerge naturally from an identified challenge, gap, or opportunity.
criteria.elements:
The answer should clearly explain the situation that requires intervention, who is affected, why it matters, and what consequences arise if no action is taken. Whenever possible, statements should be supported by evidence such as statistics, research, surveys, consultations, or other reliable sources.
A particularly effective technique is to open with a real quote, testimony, observation, or factual situation that illustrates the challenge from a human perspective. This helps the evaluator connect emotionally with the issue before moving into the evidence. Once the context has been established, statistics and other data should be used to demonstrate that the issue extends beyond individual cases and represents a broader need.
criteria.example_weak: "There is a need to improve the situation for our target group."
criteria.example_strong: "During stakeholder consultations, one participant remarked that opportunities existed but remained inaccessible in practice. This experience was echoed across multiple consultations and further confirmed by recent studies and survey findings, which identified significant barriers affecting the target group."
criteria.avoid: Avoid vague statements about needs or challenges. Avoid assuming that evaluators will automatically agree that a problem exists. Avoid making claims that are not supported by evidence. Avoid inventing quotes or relying exclusively on emotional stories without supporting data.

criteria.title: Build the Argument with Evidence
criteria.intent: Evaluators want to see that the project is based on facts and documented needs rather than assumptions. Evidence strengthens credibility and reduces the risk that the proposal appears speculative.
criteria.elements: Use statistics, research findings, surveys, stakeholder consultations, policy documents, previous project results, sector reports, or internal assessments. Evidence should support the main arguments made throughout the answer. Whenever a challenge, trend, barrier, or opportunity is mentioned, applicants should ask themselves whether there is data available to support the statement.
criteria.example_strong: "Recent studies, stakeholder consultations, and needs assessments consistently identify this issue as a growing challenge, demonstrating both its scale and the need for targeted intervention."
criteria.avoid: Avoid presenting personal opinions as evidence. Avoid including statistics without explaining their relevance. Avoid unsupported generalisations or broad statements that cannot be substantiated.

criteria.title: Explain Why Action Is Needed Now
criteria.intent: The evaluator should understand why the project is timely and why delaying action would be problematic. This helps establish urgency and relevance.
criteria.elements: Explain current developments, emerging challenges, missed opportunities, policy developments, or changing circumstances that make intervention necessary at this particular moment. The answer should demonstrate that the project responds to a present need rather than a hypothetical future concern.
criteria.example_strong: "Recent developments have increased the urgency of addressing this challenge, creating a need for coordinated action and innovative solutions at this specific moment."
criteria.avoid: Avoid presenting the issue as timeless or generic. Avoid failing to explain why the project should happen now rather than at another time.

criteria.title: Demonstrate That the Project Is the Right Response
criteria.intent: The evaluator must understand why the proposed project is an appropriate solution to the identified need.
criteria.elements: The answer should show a logical relationship between the challenge identified and the approach proposed. It should explain why the project's methodology, partnership, expertise, and planned actions are capable of addressing the situation effectively.
criteria.example_weak: "Our project will help solve the problem."
criteria.example_strong: "The project has been specifically designed in response to the identified needs and combines complementary expertise, targeted actions, and practical solutions that directly address the challenges highlighted through the needs analysis."
criteria.avoid: Avoid simply listing activities. Avoid assuming that the connection between the problem and the solution is obvious. Avoid describing what the project will do without explaining why it will work.

criteria.title: Justify Erasmus+ Funding
criteria.intent: The evaluator must be convinced that the project deserves European funding and offers value beyond the interests of the participating organisations.
criteria.elements: Explain the wider benefits of the project, its contribution to Erasmus+ objectives, the added value of cooperation, the transferability of results, and the potential for broader uptake. The answer should make clear why European investment is justified.
criteria.example_strong: "The project will generate results that can be adapted and used beyond the partnership, creating benefits for a wider community and contributing to broader European objectives."
criteria.avoid: Avoid focusing exclusively on organisational benefits. Avoid treating Erasmus+ funding simply as a source of financing rather than an investment in wider impact.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Credibility and Potential Impact
criteria.intent: Evaluators are more likely to support projects that appear realistic, achievable, and capable of delivering meaningful results.
criteria.elements: The answer should demonstrate that the consortium understands the challenge, possesses the necessary expertise, involves relevant stakeholders, and has a realistic pathway towards achieving impact. It should provide confidence that the project can deliver the outcomes it promises.
criteria.example_weak: "The project will have a strong impact."
criteria.example_strong: "The project builds on existing expertise, stakeholder engagement, and complementary capacities within the partnership, creating favourable conditions for achieving sustainable and measurable results."
criteria.avoid:
Avoid exaggerated promises, unrealistic claims, or statements that are not supported elsewhere in the application.
GOLDEN FORMULA
A strong answer to this question often follows the following sequence:
Human story, quote, or striking fact → Evidence and statistics → Explanation of the challenge → Why it matters → Why action is needed now → Why the proposed project is the right response → Why Erasmus+ should invest → Expected benefits and impact
The strongest applications balance emotion and evidence. They help evaluators understand the human reality behind the challenge while simultaneously proving, through statistics, studies, consultations, and documented needs, that the problem is real, significant, and worthy of European investment.

What are the objectives you would like to achieve and concrete results you would like to produce? How are these objectives linked to the priorities you have selected? (3000)

general_context:
This question belongs to the Relevance award criterion and focuses on the project's intended achievements. While the previous question explains why the project is needed, this question explains what the project intends to accomplish. Evaluators want to see a clear transition from identified needs and challenges to concrete objectives and measurable results.
The question has two equally important components. First, applicants must define the objectives they want to achieve and the concrete results they expect to produce. Second, they must demonstrate how these objectives are connected to the Erasmus+ priorities selected in the application. The answer should therefore not only describe ambitions, but also justify why those ambitions contribute to the chosen priorities.
writing_guidance:
This question builds directly on the needs analysis, project motivation, and selected priorities. If the previous sections identified a challenge, this section should explain what changes the project intends to create in response to that challenge.
A common mistake is to write long narrative paragraphs describing objectives in general terms. A much stronger approach is to present the objectives as a clear list of specific objectives, making them easy for evaluators to identify and assess. After presenting each objective, applicants should explain how it contributes to one or more of the selected Erasmus+ priorities and why that connection is relevant.
Whenever possible, the justification should be supported by evidence. If an objective aims to address a challenge identified through statistics, research, surveys, consultations, or policy documents, this evidence should be referenced when explaining the objective's relevance. The evaluator should understand not only what the project wants to achieve, but also why these objectives are necessary and how they respond to documented needs.
The expected results should then demonstrate what tangible outputs, improvements, or changes will emerge from achieving those objectives.
connects_to:
This question is supported by the needs analysis, project motivation, and selected priorities. The objectives should respond directly to the needs identified earlier in the application and should contribute to addressing the priorities selected by the consortium.
At the same time, this question feeds directly into the activities, work packages, deliverables, impact, and evaluation sections. Activities should explain how the objectives will be achieved. Results and outputs should demonstrate whether the objectives have been achieved. Impact should describe the changes resulting from those achievements. Therefore, the objectives serve as a bridge between the project's rationale and its implementation.
A strong proposal allows evaluators to trace a clear path from identified needs, to objectives, to activities, to results, and finally to impact.

criteria.title: Present Clear and Specific Objectives
criteria.intent: Evaluators must clearly understand what the project intends to achieve. Objectives should define the desired changes rather than simply describe activities.
criteria.elements: The answer should present a structured list of objectives rather than hiding them within long paragraphs. Each objective should be concise, specific, and understandable. Ideally, objectives should describe a desired improvement, development, strengthening, increase, enhancement, or change.
criteria.example_weak: "The project aims to support stakeholders through different activities."
criteria.example_strong: Objective 1: Strengthen specific capacities among the target group.Objective 2: Develop innovative tools and methodologies.Objective 3: Improve cooperation and knowledge exchange among stakeholders.
criteria.avoid: Avoid vague objectives. Avoid describing activities as objectives. Avoid creating objectives that are so broad that success cannot be assessed.

criteria.title: Link Each Objective to a Selected Priority
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants proof that the project's objectives directly contribute to the priorities selected in the application form.
criteria.elements:
After listing each objective, explain:
Which selected priority it contributes to.
Why that priority is relevant.
How achieving the objective will support the priority.
Why this contribution is important.
The explanation should be provided for each objective individually rather than discussing priorities only at the end of the section.
criteria.example_weak: "The objectives contribute to the selected priorities."
criteria.example_strong: Objective 1 contributes directly to Priority X because it addresses the identified challenge related to that priority. By achieving this objective, the project will help advance the specific goals promoted under the priority.
criteria.avoid: Avoid simply naming priorities without explaining the connection. Avoid assuming that the relationship between objectives and priorities is obvious.

criteria.title: Justify Objectives with Evidence
criteria.intent: Objectives should not appear arbitrary. Evaluators should understand that they were chosen in response to documented needs.
criteria.elements: Support objectives with statistics, research findings, surveys, consultations, policy documents, needs analyses, or stakeholder feedback. The evidence should explain why each objective is necessary.
criteria.example_strong: "Stakeholder consultations and recent research identified significant gaps in current capacities, justifying the need for an objective focused on strengthening knowledge and skills."
criteria.avoid: Avoid presenting objectives without explaining why they are needed. Avoid unsupported assumptions. Avoid including statistics that are unrelated to the objective being discussed.

criteria.title: Define Concrete Results
criteria.intent: Evaluators want to know what tangible outcomes will emerge from achieving the objectives.
criteria.elements:
For each objective, describe the concrete results expected. Results may include tools, methodologies, resources, learning materials, frameworks, improved competences, strengthened capacities, new practices, or measurable organisational changes.
The connection between the objective and the result should be obvious.
criteria.example_strong: "Achievement of Objective 2 will result in the development of practical resources, strengthened capacities among participants, and the adoption of new approaches within participating organisations."
criteria.avoid: Avoid describing results in vague terms. Avoid listing outputs without connecting them to objectives.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Logical Consistency
criteria.intent: The evaluator should see a coherent chain linking needs, objectives, priorities, and expected results.
criteria.elements: Each objective should clearly respond to an identified need. Each objective should contribute to a selected priority. Each result should emerge logically from the objective. The overall structure should feel coherent and interconnected.
criteria.example_weak: Objectives, priorities, and results are described separately with little connection.
criteria.example_strong: The answer demonstrates how each objective addresses a documented need, contributes to a selected priority, and produces specific results that support the project's overall goals.
criteria.avoid: Avoid treating objectives, priorities, and results as separate topics. Avoid introducing objectives that are not linked to previously identified needs.

criteria.title: Make Objectives Measurable and Achievable
criteria.intent: Evaluators are more confident in projects that present realistic and assessable objectives.
criteria.elements: Objectives should be ambitious but achievable. Whenever possible, indicate what success would look like and how progress could be measured.
criteria.example_weak: "The project will completely solve the identified challenge."
criteria.example_strong: "The project aims to contribute to addressing the challenge through specific improvements that can be monitored and evaluated during implementation."
criteria.avoid:
Avoid unrealistic promises. Avoid objectives that cannot be assessed or evaluated.
GOLDEN FORMULA
The strongest answers often follow the following structure:
Objective 1 → Why it is needed (supported by evidence/statistics) → Which priority it addresses → Why it contributes to that priority → What concrete results it will produce
Objective 2 → Why it is needed (supported by evidence/statistics) → Which priority it addresses → Why it contributes to that priority → What concrete results it will produce
Objective 3 → Why it is needed (supported by evidence/statistics) → Which priority it addresses → Why it contributes to that priority → What concrete results it will produce
And so on for each objective.
This structure makes the evaluator's job easy because they can immediately see the logic connecting needs → objectives → priorities → results. It also prevents one of the most common weaknesses in Erasmus+ applications: listing objectives without clearly explaining why they matter and how they contribute to the selected priorities.
What makes your proposal innovative? (2000)

general_context:
This question belongs to the Relevance award criterion and assesses whether the project brings something genuinely new, improved, or significantly different compared to existing approaches. Many applicants mistakenly believe that innovation means inventing something that has never existed before. In Erasmus+, innovation is much broader. A project can be innovative because it introduces a new methodology, combines existing approaches in a new way, transfers a successful practice to a new context, reaches a target group that has not been reached before, or creates a solution that improves existing practices.
The purpose of this question is not to prove that the project is revolutionary. Instead, it is to demonstrate that the project goes beyond "business as usual" and provides added value compared to what currently exists.
writing_guidance:
This question comes after the needs, priorities, objectives, and expected results have been explained. At this stage, evaluators already understand what problem the project addresses and what it aims to achieve. Now they want to understand why the proposed solution is different from existing approaches.
The strongest answers do not simply claim that the project is innovative. They prove it. Innovation should be demonstrated through evidence and comparison. Applicants should explain what currently exists, identify its limitations, and then show how their approach improves upon it.
Whenever possible, claims about innovation should be supported by evidence. Research findings, benchmarking exercises, stakeholder consultations, needs assessments, previous project experiences, evaluations of existing initiatives, and statistical data can help demonstrate that there is a gap that existing solutions do not adequately address.
A particularly effective approach is to begin with a quote, finding, observation, or stakeholder insight that highlights frustration with current approaches or reveals an unmet need. This immediately helps evaluators understand why a new or improved solution is necessary. However, as with all Erasmus+ applications, storytelling should support evidence, not replace it.
Evaluators are often more persuaded by an answer that says:
"Current approaches achieve X but fail to achieve Y, as demonstrated by recent consultations and studies. Our project addresses this gap through..."
than by an answer that simply states:
"Our project is innovative because it is unique."
connects_to:
This question is closely linked to the needs analysis, objectives, activities, and impact sections. The innovation should emerge naturally from the identified challenge and should contribute to achieving the project's objectives more effectively than existing approaches.
The innovation described here should later be visible in the activities, methodologies, outputs, and expected results. If a project claims to be innovative but then proposes conventional activities with no clear distinction from existing practice, evaluators may question its credibility.
This section also feeds directly into impact and sustainability. Genuine innovation should create benefits that can be adopted, transferred, replicated, or scaled beyond the lifetime of the project. Therefore, innovation should not be presented as an isolated feature but as a key factor that strengthens the project's potential impact.

criteria.title: Explain What Is New or Different
criteria.intent: The evaluator must clearly understand what distinguishes the project from existing approaches, practices, or solutions.
criteria.elements: The answer should identify the specific element that is innovative and explain why it is different. Innovation may lie in the methodology, partnership composition, learning approach, implementation process, target group engagement, use of technology, transfer of practices, or combination of existing solutions.
criteria.example_weak: "The project is innovative because it uses new ideas."
criteria.example_strong: "The project introduces an approach that combines previously separate practices into a single framework, creating a more effective response to the identified challenge."
criteria.avoid: Avoid claiming innovation without explaining what is actually innovative. Avoid using words such as "unique," "groundbreaking," or "revolutionary" without evidence.

criteria.title: Demonstrate the Gap in Existing Solutions
criteria.intent: Evaluators need to understand why innovation is necessary. Before explaining the new solution, explain the limitations of current approaches.
criteria.elements: Describe what currently exists, what works, what does not work, and where the gap lies. Support these observations with studies, stakeholder consultations, surveys, evaluations, benchmarking exercises, or statistics whenever possible.
criteria.example_strong: "Stakeholder consultations and previous evaluations identified significant limitations in existing approaches, particularly regarding accessibility, scalability, and user engagement. These findings highlighted the need for a different approach."
criteria.avoid: Avoid criticising existing initiatives without evidence. Avoid making unsupported claims that no solutions currently exist.

criteria.title: Prove Innovation with Evidence
criteria.intent: Innovation becomes more convincing when it is supported by evidence rather than self-declaration.
criteria.elements: Use needs assessments, surveys, stakeholder feedback, research findings, policy analyses, benchmarking exercises, previous project evaluations, and statistics to demonstrate why the proposed innovation is necessary and relevant.
criteria.example_strong: "Consultations, surveys, and recent studies consistently identified unmet needs that existing approaches have not fully addressed, creating a clear rationale for the proposed innovation."
criteria.avoid: Avoid assuming that innovation is self-evident. Avoid presenting opinions as proof of innovation.

criteria.title: Show the Added Value
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to understand not only what is different, but why being different matters.
criteria.elements: Explain how the innovation improves effectiveness, accessibility, participation, quality, efficiency, sustainability, transferability, or impact compared to existing approaches.
criteria.example_strong: "The proposed approach increases accessibility, improves engagement, and enables wider adoption, addressing several limitations identified in current practice."
criteria.avoid: Avoid describing differences without explaining their benefits.

criteria.title: Innovation Through Combination and Transfer
criteria.intent: Many applicants overlook one of the most accepted forms of Erasmus+ innovation: combining existing practices or transferring successful approaches to a new context.
criteria.elements: Explain how the project adapts, combines, transfers, or scales approaches that have proven successful elsewhere. Innovation does not always require inventing something completely new.
criteria.example_strong: "The project adapts proven approaches from different contexts and combines them into an integrated solution tailored to the identified needs."
criteria.avoid: Avoid believing that innovation only means creating something from scratch.

criteria.title: Make the Evaluator Feel the Need for Innovation
criteria.intent: Innovation becomes more persuasive when evaluators first understand why change is necessary.
criteria.elements: Consider opening with a real quote, stakeholder testimony, observation, surprising statistic, evaluation finding, or consultation result that illustrates dissatisfaction with current approaches or highlights an unmet need. Follow this immediately with evidence and explanation.
criteria.example_strong: "During consultations, stakeholders repeatedly highlighted the same challenge: existing approaches were not reaching the intended audience effectively. This observation was supported by survey findings, demonstrating the need for a different solution."
criteria.avoid: Avoid invented quotes. Avoid emotional statements that are not supported by evidence. Avoid using stories without explaining their relevance to the innovation.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Potential for Replication and Scaling
criteria.intent: Innovation is more valuable when it can benefit others beyond the immediate partnership.
criteria.elements: Explain how the innovation can be adapted, transferred, replicated, or scaled by other organisations, sectors, regions, or countries.
criteria.example_strong: "The innovation has been designed to be transferable and adaptable, allowing organisations beyond the partnership to adopt and apply the approach."
criteria.avoid:
Avoid describing innovation as useful only within the project consortium.


How is your proposal suitable for creating synergies between different fields of education, training, youth and sport or how does it have a strong potential impact on one or more of those fields? (3000)

general_context:
This question belongs to the Relevance award criterion and evaluates whether the project has the capacity to generate benefits beyond a single field or sector. Erasmus+ increasingly values projects that break traditional silos and create connections between different fields such as education, training, youth, and sport. The purpose is to determine whether the project can create added value through cross-sector collaboration, knowledge transfer, shared methodologies, or broader applicability of results.
The question contains two possible routes. A project can either demonstrate that it creates synergies between different fields, or it can demonstrate a strong impact within one or more fields. Applicants do not necessarily need to address all Erasmus+ sectors. However, they should clearly explain how the project's results, methodologies, tools, or approaches can benefit actors beyond the immediate target group and contribute to wider ecosystems.
writing_guidance:
This question appears after the project's needs, objectives, priorities, and innovation have already been explained. At this stage, evaluators understand what the project aims to achieve. They now want to understand the breadth of its relevance and impact.
Many applicants make the mistake of simply stating that their project is relevant to multiple sectors. Evaluators are not interested in declarations; they want evidence and logic. The answer should explain how knowledge, methodologies, tools, approaches, or outcomes developed within the project can be transferred, adapted, or applied in different contexts.
A strong answer often demonstrates that challenges are interconnected and do not exist exclusively within one field. For example, many challenges affect education, training, youth, sport, employment, inclusion, digitalisation, sustainability, or community development simultaneously. Projects that recognise these connections and design solutions that can be used across sectors are often perceived as having stronger European added value.
Whenever possible, applicants should support their arguments with evidence. Statistics, research findings, policy reports, stakeholder consultations, evaluations, and documented needs can help demonstrate why a cross-sector approach is necessary or why the project has potential to influence multiple fields.
An effective introduction can also include a quote, consultation finding, policy statement, or stakeholder observation highlighting the need for greater cooperation between sectors. However, as with other sections, evidence should remain the foundation of the argument.
connects_to:
This question is supported by the needs analysis, selected priorities, objectives, innovation, and partnership composition. If the project claims to generate synergies between sectors, these synergies should already be visible in the project's rationale, objectives, and planned activities.
At the same time, this section feeds directly into impact, dissemination, sustainability, and exploitation. Projects that create value across sectors often have greater opportunities for transferability, replication, stakeholder engagement, and long-term sustainability. Therefore, the argument presented here should be consistent with later descriptions of impact and use of results.
A strong answer helps evaluators understand that the project's value extends beyond its immediate participants and has the potential to influence wider systems, practices, or stakeholder communities.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Cross-Sector Relevance
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to understand whether the project addresses challenges, opportunities, or needs that are relevant to more than one field and whether the proposed solutions can be useful across different contexts.
criteria.elements: Explain which sectors or fields can benefit from the project and why. Show how the project's methodologies, resources, approaches, or results can be applied beyond the immediate target group. The focus should be on relevance and applicability rather than simply listing sectors.
criteria.example_weak: "The project is relevant to education, training, youth, and sport."
criteria.example_strong: "The project's approach can be applied in multiple settings because it addresses challenges that affect different stakeholder groups and creates resources that can be adapted to various contexts."
criteria.avoid: Avoid listing sectors without explaining the connection. Avoid claiming relevance to every field if the proposal does not genuinely support it.

criteria.title: Explain the Synergies Created
criteria.intent: If the project claims to create synergies, the evaluator must understand how different fields interact and benefit from one another through the project.
criteria.elements: Describe how expertise, practices, methodologies, resources, or experiences from different fields will be combined. Explain why collaboration between sectors creates greater value than working within a single sector.
criteria.example_strong: "The project brings together complementary expertise from different fields, allowing knowledge and practices to be exchanged and adapted, resulting in solutions that are stronger than those developed within a single sector."
criteria.avoid: Avoid assuming that partnership diversity automatically creates synergies. The benefits of collaboration must be clearly explained.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Potential for Transferability
criteria.intent: Evaluators want to know whether the project's results can be adapted and used beyond the original context.
criteria.elements: Explain how tools, methodologies, frameworks, resources, or lessons learned can be transferred to other organisations, sectors, regions, or countries. Transferability is often one of the strongest indicators of broader relevance.
criteria.example_strong: "The project's outputs have been designed to be adaptable and can therefore be used by organisations operating in different environments and sectors."
criteria.avoid: Avoid presenting results that can only be used by project partners or a very limited audience.

criteria.title: Support Claims with Evidence
criteria.intent: Cross-sector relevance and impact become more convincing when supported by evidence rather than assumptions.
criteria.elements: Use statistics, policy documents, research findings, stakeholder consultations, needs assessments, or evaluations to demonstrate why cooperation between sectors is needed or why multiple sectors face similar challenges.
criteria.example_strong: "Research, policy reports, and stakeholder consultations indicate that the identified challenge affects multiple stakeholder groups and requires coordinated responses across different sectors."
criteria.avoid: Avoid broad claims about impact without supporting evidence. Avoid making assumptions about stakeholder needs.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Broader Impact
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to understand how the project's influence extends beyond direct participants.
criteria.elements: Explain how the project can contribute to organisational practices, stakeholder cooperation, policy discussions, professional development, community engagement, or systemic improvements. Focus on the ripple effects that may emerge from the project's implementation.
criteria.example_weak: "The project will have a positive impact."
criteria.example_strong: "Beyond direct participants, the project will contribute to improved practices, stronger cooperation, and wider uptake of project results among relevant stakeholders."
criteria.avoid: Avoid limiting impact descriptions to immediate beneficiaries only.

criteria.title: Use a Systems Perspective
criteria.intent: Projects that understand how different stakeholders and sectors interact are often perceived as more strategic and sustainable.
criteria.elements: Show how the project contributes to wider ecosystems rather than isolated activities. Explain how different actors can benefit from, contribute to, or build upon the project's results.
criteria.example_weak: "The project focuses on its target group."
criteria.example_strong: "The project creates benefits not only for direct participants but also for organisations, practitioners, decision-makers, and wider stakeholder communities that can adopt and build upon the project's results."
criteria.avoid: Avoid presenting the project as an isolated intervention with no broader connections.

criteria.title: Make the Need for Synergies Tangible
criteria.intent: Evaluators are more likely to appreciate cross-sector collaboration if they first understand why it is necessary.
criteria.elements: Consider opening with a real quote, stakeholder observation, consultation finding, policy recommendation, or statistic that highlights the fragmentation of existing approaches or the need for stronger cooperation. Follow this with evidence and explanation.
criteria.example_strong: "Stakeholder consultations repeatedly highlighted that existing efforts often operate in isolation, limiting their effectiveness. This finding reinforced the need for a collaborative and cross-sector approach."
criteria.avoid:
Avoid invented quotes or unsupported observations. Avoid using emotional statements without evidence.

How does the proposal bring added value at European level through results that would not be attained by activities carried out in a single country? (2000)

general_context:
This question belongs to the Relevance award criterion and assesses one of the fundamental principles of Erasmus+ cooperation projects: European Added Value. Evaluators want to understand why the project needs a transnational partnership and why the proposed results could not be achieved, or could not be achieved as effectively, through activities implemented in only one country.
This is not a question about international participation. Simply having partners from different countries is not enough. The evaluator is looking for evidence that the cooperation itself creates value. The answer must demonstrate that the project's outcomes depend on the combination of different experiences, expertise, perspectives, practices, and contexts available through European collaboration.
A useful way to think about this question is:
"What would be lost if the project were implemented by organisations from only one country?"
If the answer is "not much," then the European added value has not been properly demonstrated.
writing_guidance:
This question comes after the project's needs, objectives, priorities, innovation, and impact have been explained. At this stage, evaluators already understand what the project intends to achieve. Now they want to understand why a European project is necessary to achieve those goals.
Many applicants make the mistake of describing the partnership instead of explaining the value of cooperation. Statements such as "the project involves partners from five countries" do not answer the question. The evaluator already knows that.
Instead, applicants should explain what each country, organisation, context, experience, or expertise contributes and how combining these elements creates results that would not emerge through a national initiative.
The strongest answers compare two scenarios:
What could realistically be achieved in a single-country project.
What becomes possible because of European cooperation.
Whenever possible, the argument should be supported by evidence. Research findings, European statistics, policy reports, stakeholder consultations, previous project experiences, or examples of differing national approaches can strengthen the case for transnational collaboration.
A compelling opening can also include a quote, consultation finding, policy recommendation, or statistic showing that the challenge exists across multiple countries. This immediately establishes why a European response may be more appropriate than a purely national one.
connects_to:
This question is closely linked to the needs analysis, objectives, partnership composition, innovation, impact, and sustainability sections.
The identified challenge should ideally affect more than one country or benefit from knowledge exchange between countries. The objectives should require expertise that no single organisation or national context can fully provide. The partnership should demonstrate complementary strengths that contribute to the project's outcomes.
At the same time, this section feeds directly into impact, dissemination, sustainability, and exploitation. Results that have European added value are often more transferable, more adaptable, and more useful across different contexts. Therefore, the argument presented here should support later claims about wider impact and long-term benefits.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Why a National Project Would Not Be Enough
criteria.intent: This is the heart of the question. The evaluator wants a direct explanation of why the project requires European cooperation.
criteria.elements:
Explain what limitations would exist if the project were implemented in only one country. Show what additional benefits become possible through transnational cooperation.
A useful writing technique is to explicitly compare:
A national approach.
The proposed European approach.
criteria.example_weak: "The project involves partners from several European countries."
criteria.example_strong: "While a national initiative could address the challenge within a single context, the proposed partnership enables the comparison of different approaches, the testing of solutions in multiple environments, and the development of results that are relevant across Europe."
criteria.avoid: Avoid describing the partnership without explaining why cooperation matters. Avoid assuming that international participation automatically creates added value.

criteria.title: Highlight Complementary Expertise
criteria.intent: Evaluators want to see that each partner contributes knowledge, experience, or capacities that strengthen the project.
criteria.elements: Explain how different expertise, methodologies, perspectives, experiences, or practices are combined to create stronger outcomes than any single organisation or country could achieve alone.
criteria.example_strong: "The partnership combines complementary expertise and experiences, allowing solutions to be co-created, tested, and refined using knowledge that would not be available within a single national context."
criteria.avoid: Avoid listing partner profiles without explaining how their expertise contributes to the project's results.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Mutual Learning and Exchange
criteria.intent: One of the strongest forms of European added value is learning from different realities, practices, and experiences.
criteria.elements: Explain how partners will exchange knowledge, compare approaches, identify good practices, and learn from one another. Show how this process improves the quality of project outputs.
criteria.example_strong: "The partnership enables the comparison of different approaches and experiences, allowing partners to identify effective practices, avoid common challenges, and co-create improved solutions."
criteria.avoid: Avoid mentioning knowledge exchange without explaining its contribution to project quality.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Transferability Across Europe
criteria.intent: Evaluators are interested in results that can be used beyond the participating organisations and countries.
criteria.elements: Explain how developing, testing, and validating results in different countries increases their relevance, adaptability, and transferability.
criteria.example_strong: "Because the project results will be developed and tested in different national contexts, they will be more adaptable and applicable to a wider range of users across Europe."
criteria.avoid: Avoid presenting results that appear useful only within one country or one organisation.

criteria.title: Link to European Challenges and Priorities
criteria.intent: The evaluator should understand that the challenge addressed is relevant beyond a local context and aligns with broader European objectives.
criteria.elements: Use European statistics, policy documents, research findings, or stakeholder consultations to demonstrate that the identified need is shared across multiple countries.
criteria.example_strong: "Research, policy documents, and stakeholder consultations demonstrate that this challenge affects multiple European countries, making transnational cooperation particularly relevant."
criteria.avoid: Avoid focusing exclusively on local circumstances without explaining the wider European relevance.

criteria.title: Explain Co-Creation Rather Than Parallel Work
criteria.intent: Evaluators want to see that partners are creating something together, not simply carrying out separate national activities.
criteria.elements: Demonstrate how project outputs will be jointly developed, improved through feedback from different countries, and shaped by diverse experiences.
criteria.example_weak: "Each partner will implement activities in its country."
criteria.example_strong: "The project outputs will be co-created through continuous collaboration, allowing different national experiences and perspectives to shape the final results."
criteria.avoid: Avoid describing the project as a collection of national actions running in parallel.

criteria.title: Use a Comparative Perspective
criteria.intent: European added value becomes stronger when applicants explicitly compare national and European approaches.
criteria.elements: Consider including a direct explanation of what the project gains through transnational cooperation that would not be possible nationally.
criteria.example_strong: "A national project could address challenges within a single context, whereas the proposed partnership enables the development of solutions that are informed by multiple realities and therefore more robust, transferable, and sustainable."
criteria.avoid:
Avoid assuming that evaluators will make this comparison themselves.

Needs analysis
What needs you want to address by implementing your project? (3000)

general_context:
Before evaluators can assess objectives, activities, innovation, impact, or sustainability, they need to understand the needs that justify the project. In simple terms, this question answers:
"What problems, gaps, challenges, opportunities, or unmet needs make this project necessary?"
A strong answer demonstrates that the project has been designed in response to clearly identified needs rather than assumptions or ideas that emerged without evidence. The evaluator should be able to see a direct connection between the needs identified here and everything that follows in the application.
writing_guidance:
This is one of the most important sections in the proposal because many subsequent questions depend on it. The objectives should address the needs identified here. The priorities should be selected because they are relevant to these needs. The activities should be designed to respond to these needs. The expected results and impact should demonstrate how these needs will be reduced or addressed.
One of the most common mistakes is writing needs as long descriptive paragraphs. Evaluators often struggle to identify the actual needs hidden inside the text. A much stronger approach is to present needs in a structured format and group them by stakeholder category.
For Youth projects, for example, needs can be presented under separate sections such as:
Youth need to:
Improve...
Access...
Develop...
Strengthen...
Youth workers need to:
Improve...
Learn...
Strengthen...
Gain...
Partner organisations need to:
Develop...
Improve...
Enhance...
Increase...
This approach makes the needs immediately visible and allows evaluators to clearly understand who needs what and why.
Most importantly, every major need should be supported by evidence whenever possible. Statistics, research findings, surveys, consultations, focus groups, policy documents, stakeholder interviews, previous project experiences, evaluations, and sector reports can all help validate the identified needs. Evaluators are much more likely to trust statements supported by evidence than general observations.
An effective introduction can also begin with a real quote, testimony, stakeholder observation, or striking statistic that illustrates the challenge from a human perspective. This helps the evaluator understand the reality behind the need before moving into the supporting evidence.
connects_to:
This question supports nearly every other section of the application.
The identified needs should directly justify:
The selected priorities.
The project objectives.
The planned activities.
The expected results.
The anticipated impact.
The sustainability strategy.
At the same time, the needs identified here become the baseline against which the project's success will later be measured. If a need is identified in this section, the evaluator should later be able to see how the project intends to address it through objectives, activities, and results.
A strong answer therefore creates a clear intervention logic:
Need → Objective → Activity → Result → Impact
If the needs are weak, unsupported, or poorly defined, the entire proposal becomes more difficult to justify.

criteria.title: Structure Needs by Target Group
criteria.intent: The evaluator must clearly understand who has which needs. Different stakeholders rarely have identical challenges, and grouping needs by target group makes the proposal easier to assess.
criteria.elements:
Present needs under separate stakeholder categories whenever possible.
For example:
Youth need to:
Develop...
Improve...
Access...
Strengthen...
Youth workers need to:
Gain...
Improve...
Learn...
Develop...
Partner organisations need to:
Enhance...
Strengthen...
Improve...
Build...
Each category should contain only needs that genuinely apply to that group.
criteria.example_weak: "The project addresses various needs related to participation, capacity building, and cooperation."
criteria.example_strong:
Youth need to:
Strengthen specific competences.
Access new opportunities.
Improve participation.
Youth workers need to:
Develop new methodologies.
Improve their professional capacities.
Partner organisations need to:
Strengthen cooperation mechanisms.
Improve the quality of services offered.
criteria.avoid: Avoid mixing all needs into one long paragraph. Avoid making evaluators search for who needs what.

criteria.title: Support Needs with Evidence
criteria.intent: Evaluators need to see that the identified needs are real and documented.
criteria.example_strong: "Stakeholder consultations, surveys, and recent research identified significant gaps affecting the target group, demonstrating the need for additional support and targeted interventions."
criteria.avoid: Avoid assumptions. Avoid statements beginning with "we believe" without supporting evidence.

criteria.title: Focus on Needs, Not Activities
criteria.intent: Many applicants confuse needs with project solutions.
criteria.elements:
This section should describe what stakeholders need, not what the project will do.
Needs describe the current situation.
Activities describe the future response.
criteria.example_strong:
"The target group needs improved competences and better access to opportunities."
The workshop is the activity that addresses the need.
criteria.avoid: Avoid presenting project activities as needs.

criteria.title: Explain Why the Needs Matter
criteria.intent: The evaluator should understand the consequences of leaving the needs unaddressed.
criteria.elements: Explain how the identified needs affect the target groups and why addressing them is important. Consider describing barriers, missed opportunities, reduced participation, lower quality outcomes, or other negative consequences.
criteria.example_strong: "If these needs remain unaddressed, stakeholders may continue facing barriers that limit participation, development, and access to opportunities."
criteria.avoid: Avoid presenting needs without explaining their significance.

criteria.title: Prioritise the Most Important Needs
criteria.intent: Evaluators prefer focused proposals over projects trying to solve everything.
criteria.elements: Identify the most relevant needs and explain why they were prioritised. The needs listed should be directly linked to the project's objectives and expected results.
criteria.example_weak: A list of twenty unrelated needs.
criteria.example_strong: A focused list of needs that clearly explain the rationale behind the project's objectives.
criteria.avoid: Avoid creating an excessive list of minor needs that later have no connection to the project.

criteria.title: Include Organisational Needs
criteria.intent: Many applicants focus only on final beneficiaries and forget the needs of partner organisations.
criteria.elements: Explain what participating organisations need in order to better support their beneficiaries. These may include needs related to capacities, methodologies, cooperation, quality improvement, innovation, outreach, or sustainability.
criteria.example_strong: Both beneficiary and organisational needs are identified and supported by evidence.
criteria.avoid: Avoid ignoring organisational needs if they are relevant to the project.

criteria.title: Humanise the Need
criteria.intent: Needs become more convincing when evaluators can connect with the reality behind them.
criteria.elements:
Consider opening with:
A real quote.
A stakeholder testimony.
A consultation finding.
A survey comment.
A striking statistic.
Then support the statement with evidence and analysis.
criteria.example_weak: "There are challenges affecting the target group."
criteria.example_strong: "During consultations, participants repeatedly highlighted barriers affecting their ability to access opportunities. These observations were later confirmed through surveys and research findings."
criteria.avoid:
Avoid invented quotes. Avoid emotional stories that are not supported by evidence.


What are the target groups of the project? How do the participating organisations engage with the project target groups in their activities?  (2000)

general_context:
This question belongs to the Relevance award criterion and focuses on two closely connected aspects of the project: who will benefit from the project and why the participating organisations are well positioned to work with those beneficiaries.
Many applicants only answer the first half of the question by describing their target groups. However, evaluators are equally interested in the second half: the relationship between the participating organisations and those target groups. They want evidence that the consortium has direct access to, experience with, and credibility among the people the project intends to reach.
In simple terms, the evaluator wants to know:
Who are the beneficiaries, and why are you the right organisations to engage them?
A strong answer demonstrates both a clear understanding of the target groups and a proven capacity to work with them effectively.
writing_guidance:
This question comes after the needs analysis and before the implementation sections. By this stage, evaluators already understand the project's rationale and objectives. Now they want to know who will be involved and whether the consortium can realistically reach them.
A common mistake is to provide only generic descriptions such as:
"Young people are our target group."
This is rarely sufficient. Evaluators want specificity. Applicants should describe target groups as precisely as possible, explaining their characteristics, challenges, needs, roles, and expected involvement in the project.
The second common mistake is describing organisations without explaining their connection to beneficiaries. Evaluators are not interested in organisational history for its own sake. They want to understand how each organisation currently engages with the target groups and what evidence demonstrates that relationship.
Whenever possible, the description should be supported by facts, statistics, membership figures, participant numbers, previous projects, service records, consultation activities, networks, partnerships, or other evidence that demonstrates access to and experience with the target groups.
An effective answer often begins with a short description of the main target groups, followed by an explanation of how each participating organisation works with them and why this makes the partnership particularly suitable for the project.
connects_to:
This question is supported by the needs analysis and project objectives. The target groups described here should be the same groups whose needs were identified earlier and whose challenges the project seeks to address.
At the same time, this section feeds directly into activities, dissemination, impact, sustainability, and evaluation. The target groups should later appear as beneficiaries of activities, users of project results, participants in project testing, and recipients of dissemination actions.
A strong answer creates consistency throughout the proposal by demonstrating that the identified needs, objectives, activities, and expected results are all focused on clearly defined target groups that the consortium is capable of reaching.

criteria.title: Clearly Define the Target Groups
criteria.intent: The evaluator must clearly understand who the project is designed for.
criteria.elements:
Present the target groups in a structured way. For each group, explain:
Who they are.
Why they are relevant to the project.
What needs or challenges they face.
How they will benefit from the project.
A useful structure is:
Primary Target Groups
(those directly involved)
Secondary Target Groups
(those indirectly benefiting)
Final Beneficiaries
(those benefiting from long-term project results)
criteria.example_weak: "Young people and organisations are the target groups."
criteria.example_strong: "The primary target groups are individuals who will directly participate in project activities and contribute to testing project results. Secondary target groups include professionals and organisations that will use and replicate project outputs. Final beneficiaries include wider communities that will benefit from improved practices and services."
criteria.avoid: Avoid broad or vague descriptions. Avoid listing target groups without explaining their role in the project.

criteria.title: Explain Why These Target Groups Were Selected
criteria.intent: The evaluator should understand why these groups are relevant to the project's objectives and needs analysis.
criteria.elements:
Link each target group to the needs identified earlier in the proposal. Explain why addressing their challenges contributes to the project's goals.
Whenever possible, support this with evidence such as research findings, consultations, surveys, statistics, policy reports, or stakeholder feedback.
criteria.example_strong: "The target groups were selected because consultations, research, and needs assessments identified them as being significantly affected by the challenges the project seeks to address."
criteria.avoid: Avoid introducing target groups that were not previously mentioned in the needs analysis.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Existing Access to the Target Groups
criteria.intent: The evaluator must be convinced that the consortium can realistically engage the intended beneficiaries.
criteria.elements:
For each participating organisation, explain:
How it currently works with the target groups.
Through which services, programmes, activities, or networks.
How many beneficiaries it reaches.
What experience it has engaging them.
The answer should provide evidence of existing relationships rather than future intentions.
criteria.example_strong: "The organisation regularly works with the target group through ongoing programmes, established networks, and previous initiatives, providing direct access to potential participants and stakeholders."
criteria.avoid: Avoid assuming access to beneficiaries without explaining how that access exists.

criteria.title: Explain the Role of Each Partner
criteria.intent: Evaluators want to understand why each partner is part of the consortium and how they contribute to engaging the target groups.
criteria.elements:
A strong approach is to dedicate a short paragraph to each partner:
Partner X engages with:
Target group(s)
Through specific activities, services, programmes, or networks
Bringing specific expertise and access
criteria.example_weak: "All partners work with the target groups."
criteria.example_strong: "Each partner brings complementary access to different segments of the target groups, ensuring broad representation, effective outreach, and meaningful engagement throughout the project."
criteria.avoid: Avoid copying organisational descriptions from partner profiles. Focus on their relationship with the target groups.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Meaningful Engagement
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to know that target groups will actively contribute to the project rather than simply receive information.
criteria.elements: Explain how target groups will participate throughout the project lifecycle.
criteria.example_strong: "Target groups will actively contribute to the development, testing, validation, and improvement of project outputs throughout implementation."
criteria.avoid: Avoid presenting target groups as passive recipients only.

criteria.title: Support Claims with Evidence
criteria.intent: Evaluators trust evidence more than assumptions.
criteria.elements:
Whenever possible, support statements with:
Number of beneficiaries reached annually.
Membership figures.
Network size.
Previous project participation.
Consultation data.
Survey results.
Stakeholder engagement statistics.
criteria.example_weak: "The organisation has strong connections with the target group."
criteria.example_strong: "The organisation engages the target group through established programmes, networks, and activities that reach a significant number of beneficiaries annually."
criteria.avoid: Avoid making claims about outreach capacity without evidence.

criteria.title: Show Diversity and Inclusion Within the Target Groups
criteria.intent: Evaluators appreciate projects that understand that target groups are not homogeneous.
criteria.elements: Where relevant, explain differences within the target groups and how the project will ensure broad representation and accessibility.
criteria.example_strong: "The project recognises the diversity within the target groups and will implement measures to ensure inclusive participation and representation."
criteria.avoid:
Avoid presenting target groups as a single uniform category if important differences exist.

How did you identify the needs of your partnership and those of your target groups? (2000)

general_context:
This question belongs to the Relevance award criterion and evaluates the quality of the project's preparation process. Unlike the previous question, which focuses on what the needs are, this question focuses on how those needs were identified. Evaluators want evidence that the partnership has not based the project on assumptions, personal opinions, or generic trends, but on a structured and credible needs assessment process.
In simple terms, the evaluator is asking:
"How do you know these are the real needs of your target groups and partner organisations?"
The strongest answers demonstrate that the identified needs emerged from research, consultations, evidence gathering, stakeholder engagement, and collaboration between partners.
writing_guidance:
This question comes after the needs and target groups have usually been described. At this point, evaluators already know what challenges the project intends to address. Now they want to understand the methodology behind those conclusions.
A common mistake is to repeat the needs themselves. This is not the purpose of the question. The focus should be on the process used to identify them.
Evaluators are generally more confident in projects that can demonstrate a structured needs assessment process. The answer should explain:
Who was consulted.
What information was collected.
How evidence was gathered.
How partners contributed.
How the final needs were validated.
The strongest applications usually combine multiple sources of information rather than relying on a single source. For example, research findings may be combined with stakeholder consultations, surveys, focus groups, interviews, previous project experiences, policy analysis, and organisational observations.
Whenever possible, applicants should mention concrete figures. For example:
Number of survey responses.
Number of consultations organised.
Number of organisations involved.
Number of stakeholders interviewed.
Number of focus groups conducted.
Specific numbers increase credibility and demonstrate a systematic approach.
An effective answer often begins by explaining the overall needs assessment process and then describes how both target group needs and partnership needs were identified and validated.
connects_to:
This question supports the needs analysis, objectives, priorities, activities, and impact sections.
The information collected through the needs assessment should explain:
Why the project is needed.
Why the selected priorities were chosen.
Why specific objectives were developed.
Why certain activities were proposed.
Why the target groups were selected.
At the same time, the quality of the needs assessment influences the credibility of the entire proposal. If evaluators believe that the needs were identified through a rigorous process, they are more likely to trust the project's rationale and intervention logic.
A strong answer therefore demonstrates that the project is evidence-driven and that its design emerged directly from the realities faced by the target groups and participating organisations.

criteria.title: Explain the Needs Assessment Process
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to understand the overall methodology used to identify needs.
criteria.elements:
Begin by explaining how the partnership approached the needs assessment process. Describe the steps taken to collect information, analyse findings, and identify common needs.
The process should appear structured and intentional rather than informal.
criteria.example_strong: "The needs assessment combined multiple sources of information, including stakeholder consultations, research, surveys, partner discussions, and analysis of existing evidence in order to identify common challenges and priorities."
criteria.avoid: Avoid vague statements such as "we know our target groups well." Evaluators want evidence of a process, not assumptions.

criteria.title: Explain How Target Group Needs Were Identified
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants proof that the target groups themselves contributed to the identification of needs.
criteria.elements:
Describe how information was collected directly from beneficiaries.
Possible methods include:
Surveys.
Interviews.
Focus groups.
Workshops.
Consultations.
Feedback sessions.
Questionnaires.
Previous project evaluations.
Ongoing organisational activities.
Whenever possible, mention participant numbers.
criteria.example_strong: "The partnership collected input directly from the target groups through consultations, surveys, and feedback activities, ensuring that the identified needs reflected their actual experiences and challenges."
criteria.avoid: Avoid presenting needs that appear to come only from organisational assumptions.

criteria.title: Explain How Partnership Needs Were Identified
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to understand why the participating organisations themselves need the project.
criteria.elements:
Describe how partners analysed their own challenges, capacity gaps, development needs, operational barriers, or opportunities for improvement.
A strong structure is:
Each partner analysed:
Current situation.
Existing strengths.
Existing gaps.
Areas requiring improvement.
Opportunities for cooperation.
The partnership then compared findings and identified common needs.
