criteria.example_strong: "Each partner conducted an internal assessment of its capacities and challenges. The results were discussed collectively and revealed several common needs that could be addressed more effectively through European cooperation."
criteria.avoid: Avoid describing partner needs without explaining how they were identified.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Use of Evidence and Statistics
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants confidence that the needs are supported by objective evidence.
criteria.elements:
Reference:
Statistics.
Research reports.
Studies.
Policy documents.
Previous project findings.
Sector reports.
Surveys.
Evaluations.
The strongest answers combine quantitative and qualitative evidence.
criteria.example_strong: "The partnership complemented stakeholder consultations with statistical data, research findings, policy analysis, and sector reports, ensuring that the identified needs were supported by both evidence and direct stakeholder input."
criteria.avoid: Avoid relying exclusively on opinions or anecdotal observations.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Joint Validation by the Partnership
criteria.intent: Evaluators want to see that the needs were not identified separately but jointly validated by the consortium.
criteria.elements:
Explain how partners compared findings, discussed results, identified common challenges, and agreed on shared priorities.
This demonstrates genuine partnership development.
criteria.example_strong: "Following the data collection phase, partners reviewed and compared findings to identify common needs, shared challenges, and opportunities for cooperation that could be addressed through the project."
criteria.avoid: Avoid presenting partner contributions as isolated exercises.

criteria.title: Explain Why a European Project Was Needed
criteria.intent: Needs assessments become stronger when they reveal common challenges across different countries or contexts.
criteria.elements: Explain how partners discovered that similar needs existed across multiple organisations, communities, or countries and why cooperation would provide a stronger response.
criteria.example_strong: "The assessment process revealed common challenges across the partnership, highlighting opportunities for mutual learning, exchange of practices, and collaborative development of solutions."
criteria.avoid: Avoid presenting the needs as purely local if the project aims to justify international cooperation.

criteria.title: Show a Bottom-Up Approach
criteria.intent: Erasmus+ evaluators generally value projects that emerge from stakeholder needs rather than organisational assumptions.
criteria.elements:
Demonstrate that beneficiaries, practitioners, stakeholders, and partners actively contributed to identifying the project's focus.
A useful phrase is:
"The project was developed through a bottom-up process that combined stakeholder input with evidence-based analysis."
criteria.example_weak: "The partnership decided on the project topic."
criteria.example_strong: "The project emerged from a structured consultation process involving target groups, stakeholders, and partner organisations, ensuring that the proposed intervention responds to real and documented needs."
criteria.avoid:
Avoid presenting the project as an idea developed exclusively by the consortium without stakeholder involvement.

How will this project address those needs? (2000)

general_context:
This question belongs to the Coherence / Design of the project logic (often still linked to Relevance and Implementation quality in Erasmus+ KA2 evaluation). It assesses whether there is a clear and realistic link between identified needs and planned project actions.
In simple terms, the evaluator is asking:
“How exactly will your project respond to the needs you have identified?”
This is not a question about describing the project in general. It is about proving causality and alignment: every important need should be matched with a concrete response mechanism inside the project.
A strong answer shows that the project is not random or activity-driven, but need-driven. Each need should clearly have a corresponding solution within the project structure (objectives, activities, outputs, results).
writing_guidance:
This question comes immediately after:
Needs of target groups and partners
Identification of those needs
Target groups definition
At this stage, evaluators already know:
What the problems are
Who is affected
How the needs were identified
Now they want to see:
What will the project actually do about it?
A common mistake is to repeat the objectives or describe the project in a general way. That is not enough. The evaluator expects a structured explanation of how needs are translated into concrete actions and results.
The strongest answers clearly demonstrate an intervention logic chain:
Need → Objective → Activity → Output → Result → Impact
This is what proves coherence.
Whenever possible, applicants should be specific:
Which activity addresses which need
Which output responds to which gap
Which result reduces which problem
Statistics can also be used here to justify the scale of intervention or expected improvement (e.g., how many participants will be reached, how many organisations will benefit, what gap size is being addressed).
connects_to:
This question is directly connected to:
Needs analysis (it must respond to it)
Objectives (they operationalise the response)
Activities (they implement the response)
Work packages (they structure the response)
Impact (they measure the response)
If this section is weak, the whole project loses coherence because evaluators cannot clearly see how the proposal moves from problem → solution.
A strong answer ensures internal consistency:
Every major need has a clear response
No activity exists without justification
No need is left unaddressed
No objective is disconnected from reality
This section therefore acts as the bridge between diagnosis (needs) and intervention (project design).

criteria.title: Link Each Need to a Concrete Response
criteria.intent: The evaluator must clearly see how each identified need is addressed through specific project actions.
criteria.elements:
For each major need, explain:
What the need is
What the project will do about it
How this action solves or reduces the need
A strong structure is:
Need → Project response → Expected change
criteria.example_weak: "The project will address the identified needs through various activities."
criteria.example_strong: "The need for improved competences will be addressed through structured learning activities, capacity-building sessions, and practical tools that enable participants to develop relevant skills and apply them in real contexts."
criteria.avoid: Avoid general statements that do not specify which need is being addressed.

criteria.title: Show Clear Intervention Logic (Need → Activity → Result)
criteria.intent: Evaluators want to see a logical chain from problem to solution.
criteria.elements:
For each need:
Identify the activity that responds to it
Explain how that activity leads to a result
Show how that result improves the situation
criteria.example_weak: "Activities will be implemented to support participants."
criteria.example_strong: "The identified need for better access to knowledge will be addressed through training activities that will result in improved competences and increased ability of participants to apply new practices in their work."
criteria.avoid: Avoid listing activities without explaining their purpose.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Coverage of All Identified Needs
criteria.intent: The evaluator must be confident that no important need has been ignored.
criteria.elements:
Show systematically that:
Each major need is addressed
No gaps remain between analysis and intervention
The project is complete in its response
A useful approach is to mirror the structure of the needs section.
criteria.example_strong: "Each identified need has been matched with a specific set of activities and outputs designed to ensure a complete and coherent response."
criteria.avoid: Avoid claiming full coverage without demonstrating it.

criteria.title: Explain the Mechanisms of Change
criteria.intent: Evaluators want to understand HOW the project creates change, not just what it does.
criteria.elements:
Explain:
How activities lead to learning, improvement, or behavioural change
How outputs become usable tools or practices
How results translate into real-world improvements
criteria.example_strong: "The project will create change by combining capacity-building activities with practical tools that enable participants to apply new knowledge directly in their professional contexts."
criteria.avoid: Avoid assuming change happens automatically.

criteria.title: Use Evidence to Justify the Response Scale
criteria.intent: Evaluators appreciate when the scale of intervention is justified.
criteria.elements:
Use:
Statistics
Needs assessment data
Research findings
Stakeholder input
to justify why the chosen activities are appropriate.
criteria.example_strong: "Based on identified needs and stakeholder data, the project will implement targeted activities designed to reach the groups most affected and ensure meaningful impact."
criteria.avoid: Avoid unsupported claims about scale or impact.

criteria.title: Ensure Alignment with Objectives and Outputs
criteria.intent: Evaluators must see coherence between needs, objectives, and activities.
criteria.elements:
Explain that:
Activities are designed to achieve objectives
Outputs are designed to respond to needs
Results are measurable and relevant
criteria.example_strong: "The planned activities are directly designed to achieve the project’s objectives by addressing the identified needs and producing concrete, measurable results."
criteria.avoid: Avoid disconnect between sections of the proposal.

criteria.title: Show Practical Application of Results
criteria.intent: Evaluators want to see that the project produces usable solutions, not theoretical outcomes.
criteria.elements:
Explain:
How outputs will be used
Who will use them
In what context they will be applied
criteria.example_strong: "The project outputs will be designed for practical application, enabling target groups and organisations to integrate them into their daily work."
criteria.avoid: Avoid abstract descriptions of outputs without usability.

criteria.title: Maintain a Needs-Based Structure (Not Activity-Based)
criteria.intent: The answer should remain structured around needs, not around project activities.
criteria.elements:
Organise the answer by:
Need 1 → response
Need 2 → response
Need 3 → response
rather than listing activities.
criteria.example_strong: Structured explanation showing how each need is addressed through multiple coordinated actions.
criteria.avoid:
Avoid turning the answer into a work package description.

Partnership and cooperation agreements
How did you form your partnership? How does the mix of participating organisations complement each other and what will be the added value of their collaboration in the framework of the project? (3000)

general_context:
This question belongs to the Quality of the Partnership / Relevance / Implementation assessment areas in Erasmus+ KA2 projects. It evaluates how and why the consortium was built, whether it is strategically composed, and whether the organisations involved genuinely complement each other in order to achieve the project objectives.
In simple terms, the evaluator is asking:
“Why these partners, and why does this specific combination of organisations make the project stronger?”
This is not a descriptive question about listing partners. It is a strategic question about partnership logic: how the consortium was formed, what each partner contributes, and why the project would not be as effective without this specific mix of organisations.
A strong answer demonstrates that the partnership is not accidental or administrative, but purposefully designed based on needs, expertise, roles, and added value.
writing_guidance:
This question usually appears after the needs, objectives, and target groups have already been described. At this stage, evaluators already understand what the project wants to achieve. Now they want to understand who will deliver it and why these organisations are the right ones.
A common mistake is to describe partners individually without explaining the logic of their cooperation. Another mistake is to simply state that “partners have experience in the topic” without explaining how this experience complements others.
The strongest answers show:
How the partnership was built (process)
Why each partner was selected (criteria) and check each partners’ expertise is represented within the project team.
When preparing this section, review the ESTA INFORMACION ESTA DISPONIBLE EN LE PERFIL DE LA ENTIDAD XX Y LA SECCIÓN DE ESCRIBIR "CONSORCIO"  PIF for their expertise and to adapt how it will contribute to the project
How partners complement each other (roles and expertise)
What added value emerges from collaboration (synergy)
Whenever possible, this should be supported with evidence such as:
Previous cooperation history
Existing networks or projects
Sectoral experience
Statistics on reach or impact
Organisational capacity and expertise
An effective answer often begins with the formation logic, then moves to complementarity, and ends with added value and synergy.
connects_to:
This question is directly connected to:
Needs analysis (why certain expertise is required)
Objectives (what capacity is needed to achieve them)
Activities (who will implement what)
Work packages (division of roles)
Impact and sustainability (quality of implementation depends on partnership strength)
A weak partnership logic usually leads to weak coherence in implementation. A strong partnership explanation therefore strengthens the credibility of the entire proposal.
The evaluator should clearly see that:
Each partner is necessary
No partner is redundant
The combination creates value that no single organisation could achieve alone

criteria.title: Explain How the Partnership Was Formed
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to understand the origin and logic of the consortium.
criteria.elements:
Explain:
How partners were identified
Why they were invited
What criteria were used (expertise, access to target groups, geographical balance, experience, etc.)
Whether there was previous cooperation
A strong formation story shows intentional selection based on project needs.
criteria.example_weak: "The partnership includes organisations from different countries."
criteria.example_strong: "The partnership was formed based on complementary expertise, previous collaboration experience, and shared interest in addressing the identified needs of the target groups."
criteria.avoid: Avoid presenting the partnership as random or purely administrative.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Complementarity Between Partners
criteria.intent: Evaluators must see that each partner brings something different and necessary.
criteria.elements:
Explain how partners complement each other in terms of:
Expertise
Experience
Sectoral knowledge
Access to target groups
Methodological capacity
Geographic or cultural perspective
criteria.example_weak: "All partners have experience in the field."
criteria.example_strong: "The partnership brings together complementary expertise, combining organisations with different but aligned strengths that allow the project to address the identified needs from multiple perspectives."
criteria.avoid: Avoid repeating partner descriptions without showing how they fit together.

criteria.title: Describe the Role of Each Partner in the Project
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to see that roles are clearly defined and logically distributed.
criteria.elements:
For each partner, explain:
Their role in the project
Their responsibility in implementation
Their contribution to outputs or activities
Their access to target groups or expertise
A structured “Partner → Role → Contribution” format is highly effective.
criteria.example_weak: "Each partner will contribute to the project."
criteria.example_strong: "Each partner has a clearly defined role aligned with their expertise, ensuring efficient implementation and high-quality results."
criteria.avoid: Avoid unclear or overlapping responsibilities.

criteria.title: Show Added Value of Collaboration
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to understand why working together is better than working individually.
criteria.elements:
Explain:
What the partnership achieves collectively
Why results would be weaker without cooperation
What synergies emerge from collaboration
Focus on:
Quality improvement
Innovation
Transferability
Efficiency
Wider impact
criteria.example_strong: "The collaboration creates added value by combining diverse expertise and perspectives, resulting in more comprehensive, transferable, and impactful outcomes than would be possible through isolated national efforts."
criteria.avoid: Avoid general statements without explanation of why value is added.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Synergy Between Organisations
criteria.intent: Evaluators want evidence that partners interact in a meaningful and productive way.
criteria.elements:
Explain how:
Knowledge is exchanged
Practices are shared
Co-creation happens
Joint development improves outputs
criteria.example_strong: "The partnership enables continuous exchange of knowledge and practices, ensuring that outputs are co-created, tested, and improved through the combined expertise of all organisations."
criteria.avoid: Avoid describing partners as working in isolation.

criteria.title: Link Partnership Composition to Project Objectives
criteria.intent: Evaluators must see alignment between who is involved and what the project aims to achieve.
criteria.elements:
Explain:
Why each partner is necessary for specific objectives
How their expertise supports project goals
How the consortium structure ensures success
criteria.example_strong: "Each partner contributes directly to the achievement of project objectives by bringing specific expertise and capacities that ensure effective implementation."
criteria.avoid: Avoid disconnect between objectives and partner roles.

criteria.title: Explain Trust, Experience, or Previous Cooperation (if applicable)
criteria.intent: Previous cooperation strengthens credibility and efficiency.
criteria.elements:
Mention:
Past projects together
Existing networks
Long-term collaboration
Shared experience
criteria.example_strong: "Where applicable, partners have previously collaborated, which strengthens coordination, trust, and implementation efficiency."
criteria.avoid: Avoid inventing cooperation history if it does not exist.

criteria.title: Show Geographic and Contextual Diversity
criteria.intent: European projects benefit from diversity of contexts.
criteria.elements:
Explain how different national or regional contexts:
Enrich perspectives
Improve adaptability
Strengthen transferability
criteria.example_strong: "The geographical diversity of the partnership contributes to a broader understanding of the identified challenges and increases the transferability of project results."
criteria.avoid:
Avoid treating geography as automatically valuable without explanation.

What is the task allocation and how does it reflect the commitment and active contribution of all participating organisations? (3000)

connects_to:
This question is directly connected to:
-Partnership quality
-Project implementation
-Cooperation arrangements
-Quality assurance
-Impact and sustainability
A balanced allocation of responsibilities demonstrates that the consortium has the capacity, commitment and collaborative approach necessary to successfully implement the project.

criteria.title: Explain the Principles of Task Allocation
criteria.intent: Evaluators want to understand how responsibilities have been distributed across the partnership and whether the allocation is based on the strengths of each organisation.
criteria.avoid: Avoid suggesting that tasks were divided equally without considering the specific strengths of each organisation.

criteria.title: Demonstrate the Commitment and Active Contribution of All Partners
criteria.intent: Evaluators want evidence that every organisation is actively engaged throughout the project and shares responsibility for its success.
criteria.avoid:
Avoid presenting some organisations as passive contributors or limiting their involvement to only one phase of the project.

Describe the mechanisms for coordination and communication between the participating organisations, as well as with other relevant stakeholders, in particular with the use of educational platforms (for example, School Education platform including eTwinning and Erasmus+ space on EPALE).* 3000

general_context:
This question belongs to the Quality of Project Design and Implementation criterion in Erasmus+ KA2 projects. It assesses whether the partnership has a clear, realistic, and structured system for coordination, communication, and collaboration both internally (between partners) and externally (with stakeholders).
In simple terms, the evaluator is asking:
“How will you ensure that all partners actually work together effectively, stay coordinated, and communicate in a structured way throughout the project?”
This is not a question about general communication habits or informal collaboration. It is about the operational system of cooperation that ensures the project runs smoothly, decisions are made efficiently, responsibilities are clear, and stakeholders are meaningfully involved.
A strong answer demonstrates that communication is not occasional or ad hoc, but planned, documented, and supported by tools and platforms, including Erasmus+ digital environments such as eTwinning and EPALE where relevant.
writing_guidance:
This question comes at a stage where evaluators already know:
What the project is about
Who the partners are
What needs are addressed
What objectives and activities are planned
Now they want to understand how the partnership will function in practice on a day-to-day basis.
A very common weakness in applications is describing communication in very general terms such as:
“We will communicate regularly”
“We will hold meetings”
“We will ensure coordination”
These statements are not sufficient. Evaluators expect a clear governance and communication architecture, including:
Who communicates with whom
How often communication takes place
Which tools are used
How decisions are made
How information flows between partners
How external stakeholders are involved
How Erasmus+ platforms are integrated into the project workflow
Whenever possible, the answer should distinguish between:
Operational communication (daily/weekly coordination)
Strategic communication (decision-making and steering)
External communication (stakeholders, dissemination, platforms)
Evidence of structure and discipline is essential.
connects_to:
This question is directly connected to:
Partnership quality and roles
Work package management
Implementation efficiency
Risk management
Quality assurance
Dissemination and exploitation
Sustainability of results
A strong communication system ensures that:
Activities are implemented consistently across countries
Outputs are developed collaboratively
Quality is monitored effectively
Stakeholders are engaged throughout the process
Results are properly disseminated and used
If communication is weak, the entire project risks fragmentation, delays, inconsistent outputs, and reduced impact. Therefore, evaluators treat this section as a key indicator of project feasibility and professionalism.

criteria.title: Describe Internal Coordination Structure
criteria.intent: The evaluator must understand how coordination between partners is organised in a structured and reliable way.
criteria.elements:
Explain:
Who coordinates the project (lead partner)
Whether there is a steering committee or coordination group
How responsibilities are distributed
How decisions are made
How often coordination meetings take place
criteria.example_strong: "The project will be coordinated through a structured management system led by the coordinating organisation, supported by a steering group composed of all partners to ensure joint decision-making and monitoring."
criteria.avoid: Avoid vague descriptions without structure or governance clarity.

criteria.title: Define Communication Channels and Tools
criteria.intent: Evaluators want to see practical tools that ensure smooth day-to-day communication.
criteria.elements:
Specify:
Online tools (e.g., email, cloud platforms, project management tools)
Document sharing systems
Communication frequency
File management system
Real-time collaboration tools
criteria.example_strong: "Partners will use structured digital communication tools, including shared online platforms for document management, regular email coordination, and collaborative tools to ensure transparency and efficiency."
criteria.avoid: Avoid listing tools without explaining their function.

criteria.title: Explain Decision-Making Process
criteria.intent: The evaluator needs to understand how decisions are taken and who is responsible.
criteria.elements:
Describe:
Whether decisions are consensus-based
Role of the coordinator
Role of steering group
How conflicts are resolved
How strategic decisions are validated
criteria.example_strong: "Strategic decisions will be taken by the steering group through consensus, while operational decisions will be managed by the coordinating organisation in close consultation with partners."
criteria.avoid: Avoid unclear or unrealistic decision-making structures.

criteria.title: Describe Communication with Target Groups and Stakeholders
criteria.intent: Evaluators want to see that communication goes beyond the consortium.
criteria.elements:
Explain:
How target groups are informed and involved
How stakeholders are consulted
How feedback is collected
How external actors contribute
criteria.example_weak: "Stakeholders will be informed about the project."
criteria.example_strong: "Target groups and stakeholders will be actively engaged through structured consultation activities, feedback mechanisms, and participation in project development and validation phases."
criteria.avoid: Avoid treating stakeholders as passive recipients of information.

criteria.title: Integrate Erasmus+ Digital Platforms
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to see added value from Erasmus+ ecosystem tools.
criteria.elements:
Explain how platforms will be used:
eTwinning (school collaboration, if relevant)
EPALE or SALTO Youth (adult learning / VET / youth work community, depends if relevant)
Erasmus+ Project Results Platform (dissemination)
Online communities for sharing results
criteria.example_strong: "The project will make use of Erasmus+ digital platforms such as EPALE and, where relevant, eTwinning to facilitate dissemination, peer learning, and exchange of practices across countries."
criteria.avoid: Avoid mentioning platforms without explaining how they will be used.

criteria.title: Ensure Regularity and Predictability of Communication
criteria.intent: Evaluators want assurance that communication is continuous and structured.
criteria.elements:
Explain:
Frequency of meetings (monthly, quarterly, etc.)
Reporting cycles
Milestone-based communication
Interim updates
criteria.example_strong: "The partnership will ensure regular communication through scheduled meetings, structured reporting cycles, and milestone-based coordination to guarantee consistent progress monitoring."
criteria.avoid: Avoid vague or irregular communication plans.

criteria.title: Explain Quality Assurance Through Communication
criteria.intent: Communication is also a quality control mechanism.
criteria.elements:
Describe:
How feedback is shared
How issues are identified
How improvements are discussed
How monitoring is done collaboratively
criteria.example_strong: "Communication structures will support continuous quality assurance by enabling regular feedback exchange, joint reflection, and collective problem-solving."
criteria.avoid: Avoid separating communication from quality management.

criteria.title: Show Transparency and Documentation
criteria.intent: Evaluators value traceable communication.
criteria.elements:
Explain:
Meeting minutes
Shared documentation
Reporting systems
Transparent information flow
criteria.example_strong: "All coordination activities will be documented through meeting minutes, shared repositories, and structured reporting tools to ensure transparency and accountability."
criteria.avoid:
Avoid mentioning documentation without systems.

 Project design and implementation
Work Package 1 – Project Management

How will the progress, quality and achievement of project activities be monitored? Please give information about the involved staff, as well as the timing and frequency of the monitoring activities. (3000)-

general_context:
This question belongs to the Project Management / Quality Assurance section of Erasmus+ KA2 applications. It evaluates whether the project has a clear, structured, and realistic system for monitoring progress, quality, and results throughout implementation.
In simple terms, the evaluator is asking:
“How will you make sure the project is on track, delivering good quality results, and achieving what it promised?”
This is not about describing activities or objectives again. It is about explaining the internal monitoring system that ensures the project is controlled, adjusted when needed, and continuously improved.
A strong answer demonstrates that monitoring is not occasional or informal, but planned, continuous, and shared across the partnership, involving defined staff roles, clear responsibilities, and fixed timelines.
Evaluators expect a system that covers three dimensions:
Progress monitoring (are activities implemented on time?)
Quality monitoring (are outputs meeting standards?)
Impact monitoring (are results aligned with objectives?)
writing_guidance:
This question is part of Work Package 1 – Project Management, meaning it is foundational for the entire project. If monitoring is weak, the credibility of all other work packages is reduced.
At this stage, evaluators already know:
What the project will do
Who will do it
What outputs are expected
Now they want to understand:
How you will ensure everything actually happens as planned and at the required quality level
A common mistake is to describe general coordination meetings without explaining monitoring structure, tools, or responsibilities.
Another mistake is focusing only on reporting to the European Commission. Monitoring is broader: it includes internal quality control, continuous tracking, and corrective actions.
A strong answer should clearly describe:
Who is responsible for monitoring
What exactly is monitored
How often monitoring takes place
What tools or methods are used
How results are documented and used for improvement
Whenever possible, it should also include:
Specific roles (project manager, quality manager, WP leaders, etc.)
Regular intervals (monthly, quarterly, per milestone)
Structured instruments (checklists, KPIs, reporting templates, evaluation grids)
connects_to:
This question is directly connected to:
Work Package implementation
Quality assurance system
Risk management
Communication mechanisms
Evaluation strategy
Impact measurement
A strong monitoring system ensures:
Activities are implemented on time
Outputs meet expected quality standards
Deviations are identified early
Corrective actions are applied
Final results are credible and transferable
If monitoring is weak, the project risks delays, inconsistent outputs, and reduced impact. Therefore, evaluators see this section as a key indicator of project reliability and professionalism.

criteria.title: Define the Overall Monitoring System
criteria.intent: The evaluator must understand the structure of the monitoring system.
criteria.elements:
Explain:
That monitoring is continuous and structured
That it covers progress, quality, and results
That it is integrated into Work Package 1 (Project Management)
criteria.example_strong: "The progress, quality, and achievement of project activities will be continuously monitored through a structured project management system ensuring timely implementation and high-quality results across all work packages."
criteria.avoid: Avoid vague statements without describing structure or scope.

criteria.title: Identify Responsible Staff and Roles
criteria.intent: Evaluators want to know who is responsible for monitoring.
criteria.elements:
Define roles such as:
Project coordinator (overall monitoring)
Project manager (day-to-day tracking)
Work package leaders (task-level monitoring)
Quality manager (quality control)
Financial/admin staff (if relevant)
criteria.example_strong: "Monitoring will be coordinated by the project coordinator, supported by WP leaders responsible for tracking progress within their respective work packages, ensuring distributed and shared responsibility."
criteria.avoid: Avoid unclear or collective responsibility without structure.

criteria.title: Explain What Will Be Monitored
criteria.intent: The evaluator must understand the scope of monitoring.
criteria.elements:
Cover:
Activity progress (timelines, milestones)
Output development (deliverables, products)
Quality standards (consistency, relevance, usability)
Partner performance
Budget implementation (if relevant)
criteria.example_strong: "Monitoring will cover the progress of activities, the quality of outputs, adherence to timelines, and achievement of project milestones across all work packages."
criteria.avoid: Avoid limiting monitoring to only one dimension (e.g. just activities).

criteria.title: Describe Monitoring Tools and Methods
criteria.intent: Evaluators want practical evidence of how monitoring will be done.
criteria.elements:
Mention tools such as:
Progress tracking sheets
KPIs (Key Performance Indicators)
Milestone checklists
Online dashboards
Reporting templates
Internal evaluation grids
Shared digital platforms
criteria.example_weak: "We will use tools to monitor progress."
criteria.example_strong: "Monitoring will be supported by structured tools such as progress tracking templates, milestone checklists, and shared digital platforms for continuous reporting and documentation."
criteria.avoid: Avoid listing tools without explaining their purpose.

criteria.title: Define Timing and Frequency of Monitoring
criteria.intent: Evaluators need to see that monitoring is regular and predictable.
criteria.elements:
Specify:
Monthly progress checks
Quarterly reports
Milestone-based reviews
Mid-term evaluation
Final evaluation
criteria.example_strong: "Monitoring activities will be conducted on a monthly basis for operational tracking, with quarterly progress reviews and formal milestone-based assessments throughout the project lifecycle."
criteria.avoid: Avoid vague frequency descriptions.

criteria.title: Include Quality Assurance Mechanisms
criteria.intent: Monitoring is not only about progress but also about quality.
criteria.elements:
Explain:
Peer review among partners
Internal quality checks
Feedback loops
Testing of outputs
Validation processes
criteria.example_strong: "Quality will be ensured through continuous internal review processes, peer feedback among partners, and structured validation of outputs before finalisation."
criteria.avoid: Avoid separating quality from monitoring.

criteria.title: Explain How Monitoring Data Will Be Used
criteria.intent: Monitoring must lead to action, not just reporting.
criteria.elements:
Describe:
How issues will be identified
How corrective actions will be taken
How improvements will be implemented
How decisions will be adjusted
criteria.example_strong: "Monitoring results will be systematically analysed and used to implement corrective actions, improve implementation processes, and ensure achievement of project objectives."
criteria.avoid: Avoid monitoring without follow-up action.

criteria.title: Link Monitoring to Reporting Obligations
criteria.intent: Evaluators expect alignment with Erasmus+ reporting requirements.
criteria.elements:
Explain:
Internal reporting cycles
Interim reports
Final reports
Coordination with EU reporting
criteria.example_strong: "Monitoring activities will feed into structured internal and external reporting cycles, ensuring transparency and compliance with Erasmus+ reporting requirements."
criteria.avoid:
Avoid focusing only on administrative reporting.

How will you ensure proper budget control and time management in your project? (3000)

writing_guidance:
Evaluators already understand the project's objectives, activities and partnership. They now want to assess whether the consortium has the management capacity to deliver the project successfully.
Weak applications often rely on general statements such as:
"The coordinator will monitor the budget."
"Partners will meet deadlines."
Instead, applicants should explain how financial and time management will be organised, monitored and adjusted throughout implementation.
connects_to:
This question supports:
Project management
Risk management
Quality assurance
Efficient implementation
Sustainability
Strong financial and time management increases confidence that the project can achieve its objectives while using Erasmus+ funding responsibly.

criteria.title: Structured Financial and Time Management
criteria.intent: Evaluators want to understand how financial resources and timelines will be planned, monitored and coordinated.
criteria.avoid: Avoid describing financial management as the coordinator's responsibility only.

criteria.title: Continuous Monitoring and Corrective Measures
criteria.intent: Evaluators expect evidence that progress is continuously monitored and deviations are addressed promptly.
criteria.avoid:
Avoid describing monitoring without explaining how it supports decision-making.

What are your plans for handling risks for project implementation (e.g. delays, budget, conflicts, etc.)? (2000)

connects_to:
This question supports:
Project management
Partnership cooperation
Quality assurance
Sustainability
Effective risk management contributes directly to successful project delivery.

criteria.title: Risk Prevention and Mitigation
criteria.intent: Evaluators want to understand how risks will be identified and prevented before they affect implementation.
criteria.avoid: Avoid presenting risk management as reactive.

criteria.title: Monitoring and Collaborative Response
criteria.intent: Evaluators expect all partners to contribute to identifying and resolving implementation challenges.
criteria.avoid:
Avoid assigning responsibility for risk management exclusively to one organisation.

How will you ensure that the activities are designed in an accessible and inclusive way? (3000)

general_context:
This question belongs to the Relevance and Quality of Project Design and Implementation criteria. It evaluates whether the project ensures equal opportunities for participation and removes barriers that may prevent young people from fully benefiting from project activities.
In simple terms, the evaluator is asking:
"How will you make sure that everyone can participate and benefit from the project regardless of their background or circumstances?"
A strong answer demonstrates that accessibility and inclusion are integrated into the design, implementation and evaluation of all activities.
writing_guidance:
In Erasmus+ Youth, inclusion is a horizontal priority.
Evaluators expect practical measures rather than general commitments, demonstrating how activities will accommodate participants with different needs, abilities and backgrounds.
connects_to:
This question supports:
Inclusion and diversity
Quality of activities
Impact
Participation
Inclusive project design strengthens both the quality and reach of project results.

criteria.title: Inclusive Activity Design
criteria.intent: Evaluators want to understand how accessibility is integrated into the planning of project activities.
criteria.avoid: Avoid describing inclusion as a general principle without practical measures.

criteria.title: Support for Equal Participation
criteria.intent: Evaluators want evidence that participants with fewer opportunities receive appropriate support.
criteria.avoid:
Avoid assuming that equal treatment automatically results in equal participation.


How does the project incorporate the use of digital tools and learning methods to complement the physical activities and to improve cooperation between partner organisations? (3000)

general_context:
This question belongs to the Quality of Project Design and Implementation criterion. It evaluates how digital technologies are integrated into the project to enhance learning, support cooperation between partners and complement face-to-face activities.
In simple terms, the evaluator is asking:
"How will digital tools strengthen both the learning experience and cooperation within the partnership?"
The emphasis is not on the number of digital tools used, but on how they contribute to achieving the project's objectives.
A strong answer demonstrates that digital tools support collaboration, communication, learning and long-term accessibility of project results.
writing_guidance:
Evaluators expect digitalisation to be integrated throughout the project rather than limited to online meetings.
Digital tools should enhance cooperation, facilitate blended learning and increase the accessibility and sustainability of project outcomes.
connects_to:
This question supports:
Project implementation
Partnership cooperation
Innovation
Dissemination
Sustainability
Meaningful use of digital tools improves both the efficiency of cooperation and the educational value of project activities.

criteria.title: Digital Tools for Cooperation
criteria.intent: Evaluators want to understand how digital technologies support day-to-day collaboration between partners.
criteria.avoid: Avoid simply listing digital tools without explaining their purpose.

criteria.title: Blended Learning and Digital Participation
criteria.intent: Evaluators expect digital methods to complement, rather than replace, face-to-face activities.
criteria.avoid:
Avoid presenting digital tools as an end in themselves rather than as instruments that improve learning and cooperation.

How does the project incorporate green practices in different project phases? (3000)

general_context:
This question belongs to the Erasmus+ horizontal priority on Environment and Fight Against Climate Change and is usually assessed under the Relevance and Quality of Project Design and Implementation criteria.
In simple terms, the evaluator is asking:
“How will environmental sustainability be integrated into the way the project is designed, managed, implemented, communicated, and sustained?”
This is not a question about whether the project topic is environmental. Even projects that have no environmental theme are expected to demonstrate how they will operate in a more sustainable and environmentally responsible way.
A strong answer shows that green practices are integrated throughout the project lifecycle and are not limited to a few isolated actions such as using less paper or organising online meetings.
The evaluator wants to see that sustainability has been considered in project management, mobility, events, communication, procurement, dissemination, and long-term use of project results.
writing_guidance:
Many applicants misunderstand this question and start describing environmental challenges or environmental objectives. Unless the project specifically focuses on environmental issues, that is usually not what the evaluator is asking.
The focus is on:
How the project itself will be implemented in a sustainable way.
The strongest answers demonstrate that environmental considerations have been integrated into different phases of the project.
A useful way to think about the question is:
How will we reduce environmental impact?
How will we promote sustainable behaviours?
How will we make environmentally responsible choices?
How will we encourage partners and participants to adopt greener practices?
Evaluators generally appreciate practical measures rather than generic statements about sustainability.
connects_to:
This question is connected to:
Project management.
Mobility activities.
Meetings and events.
Dissemination.
Procurement.
Communication.
Sustainability of results.
A strong answer shows that environmental considerations are embedded throughout the project rather than treated as an isolated activity.
The project should demonstrate consistency betwee planning, implementation, evaluation, long-term exploitation of results.
Evaluators should finish reading with confidence that environmental responsibility is integrated into the project's operational culture.

criteria.title: Itegrate Green Practices from the Beginning
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to see that sustainability is considered from the planning stage and not added later.
criteria.elements: Explain how environmental considerations will be integrated into planning, decision-making, activity design, resource allocation.
criteria.example_strong: "Environmental considerations will be integrated from the planning phase, ensuring that project decisions, activities, and resource use are aligned with sustainable practices."
criteria.avoid: Avoid making sustainability appear as an afterthought.

criteria.title: Implement Sustainable Project Management
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to understand how daily project management will reduce environmental impact.
criteria.elements:
Possible examples include:
Digital document management.
Paperless administration.
Electronic signatures.
Cloud-based collaboration.
Digital reporting systems.
Online coordination tools.
criteria.example_strong: "The project will adopt digital management systems, minimising paper consumption through electronic documentation, digital signatures, cloud-based collaboration, and online reporting mechanisms."
criteria.avoid: Avoid mentioning digitalisation without linking it to environmental benefits.

criteria.title: Apply Green Practices to Meetings and Mobility
criteria.intent: Travel is often one of the largest environmental impacts in Erasmus+ projects.
criteria.elements:
Explain how the partnership will:
Prioritise online meetings when appropriate.
Reduce unnecessary travel.
Encourage sustainable transport options.
Optimise travel planning.
Combine activities where possible.
criteria.example_strong: "The partnership will reduce environmental impact by combining online and physical meetings, minimising unnecessary travel and encouraging the use of more sustainable transport options whenever feasible."
criteria.avoid: Avoid claiming sustainability while planning excessive travel.

criteria.title: Ensure Environmentally Responsible Events and Activities
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to see sustainability reflected in project implementation.
criteria.example_strong: "Project events will prioritise digital materials, minimise waste generation, encourage responsible resource use, and apply environmentally conscious organisational practices."
criteria.avoid: Avoid broad statements without practical measures.

criteria.title: Promote Sustainable Behaviour Among Participants
criteria.intent: The project should encourage participants to adopt environmentally responsible practices.
criteria.elements:
Explain how participants will be encouraged to:
Use digital resources.
Reduce waste.
Travel responsibly.
Adopt sustainable habits during activities.
criteria.example_strong: "Participants will be encouraged to adopt environmentally responsible behaviours throughout project activities through awareness-raising and practical sustainability measures."
criteria.avoid: Avoid passive awareness-only approaches.

criteria.title: Include Green Practices in Dissemination and Communication
criteria.intent: Dissemination should also reflect sustainability principles.
criteria.example_strong: "The project will prioritise digital dissemination channels, reducing the need for printed materials while ensuring broad accessibility of project results."
criteria.avoid: Avoid large-scale printing without justification.

criteria.title: Integrate Green Procurement and Resource Use
criteria.intent: Evaluators appreciate responsible use of resources.
criteria.elements:
Where relevant, explain:
Sustainable purchasing decisions.
Preference for environmentally friendly materials.
Efficient use of resources.
Waste minimisation.
criteria.example_strong: "The partnership will seek environmentally responsible solutions when procuring materials and organising project activities, aiming to minimise resource consumption and waste."
criteria.avoid: Avoid mentioning procurement without environmental considerations.

criteria.title: Monitor and Review Green Practices
criteria.intent: Environmental sustainability should be monitored like other project objectives.
criteria.elements:
Explain how partners will:
Review environmental measures.
Monitor implementation of green practices.
Identify improvements during the project.
criteria.example_strong: "The partnership will periodically review the implementation of sustainability measures and identify opportunities for continuous improvement throughout the project lifecycle."
criteria.avoid:
Avoid presenting sustainability as a one-time action.


How does the project encourage participation and civic engagement in different project phases? (3000)

general_context:
This question belongs primarily to the Relevance and Quality of Project Design and Implementation criteria. It evaluates whether the project actively involves its target groups and stakeholders throughout the project lifecycle and whether it creates opportunities for meaningful participation, ownership, engagement, and contribution.
In simple terms, the evaluator is asking:
“How will people be actively involved in the project, rather than simply receiving its results?”
The focus is not only on participation in activities, but on participation in the design, implementation, testing, evaluation, dissemination, and sustainability of the project.
The second element of the question is civic engagement. Erasmus+ generally values projects that encourage individuals to become more active contributors within their communities, organisations, networks, or society. The evaluator wants to understand how the project creates opportunities for people to express opinions, contribute ideas, participate in decision-making, collaborate with others, and become more engaged citizens and stakeholders.
A strong answer demonstrates that participation is embedded throughout the project and that beneficiaries are not passive recipients but active contributors.
writing_guidance:
This question appears after the project objectives, needs, target groups, and activities have been defined.
At this point, evaluators already know:
What the project wants to achieve.
Who the target groups are.
What activities will be implemented.
Now they want to understand:
How the project will involve people in a meaningful way throughout the entire project lifecycle.
Many applicants make the mistake of focusing only on participation in events, workshops, or training activities.
However, participation in Erasmus+ is much broader and may include:
Consultation.
Co-creation.
Decision-making.
Testing.
Feedback.
Peer learning.
Volunteering.
Community involvement.
Dissemination activities.
Sustainability actions.
The strongest answers show participation in every major phase of the project rather than limiting it to implementation activities.
Whenever possible, applicants should explain:
Who participates.
When they participate.
How they participate.
What influence they have on the project.
connects_to:
This question is closely connected to:
Needs analysis.
Target groups.
Project activities.
Impact.
Dissemination.
Sustainability.
Participation often starts during project preparation and continues through:
Development.
Testing.
Validation.
Evaluation.
Exploitation of results.
Strong participation mechanisms increase:
Relevance.
Ownership.
Motivation.
Sustainability.
Long-term impact.
A strong answer demonstrates that engagement is not an isolated activity but an integral element of project implementation.

criteria.title: Ensure Participation Throughout All Project Phases
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to see that participation is continuous and not limited to a single activity or event.
criteria.elements:
Describe participation during:
Preparation.
Implementation.
Testing.
Evaluation.
Dissemination.
Sustainability.
A useful structure is to explain participation phase by phase.
criteria.example_strong: "Target groups will be actively involved throughout the project lifecycle, contributing to the design, implementation, testing, evaluation, and dissemination of project results."
criteria.avoid: Avoid presenting participation only as attendance.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Co-Creation and Ownership
criteria.intent: Evaluators value projects where beneficiaries help shape the project rather than simply receive services.
criteria.elements:
Explain how participants will:
Provide input.
Share ideas.
Contribute to development.
Validate outputs.
Influence improvements.
criteria.example_strong: "Participants will actively contribute to the development and refinement of project outputs through consultations, feedback processes, testing activities, and co-creation opportunities."
criteria.avoid: Avoid presenting beneficiaries as passive users only.

criteria.title: Explain How Civic Engagement Will Be Encouraged
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to understand how the project promotes active involvement in communities and society.
criteria.elements:
Explain how the project encourages participants to:
Express opinions.
Participate in discussions.
Collaborate with others.
Contribute to community initiatives.
Engage with stakeholders.
Participate in decision-making processes.
Become active contributors within their environments.
criteria.example_strong: "The project will create opportunities for participants to engage in dialogue, contribute ideas, collaborate with stakeholders, and take an active role in shaping initiatives relevant to their communities and organisations."
criteria.avoid: Avoid mentioning civic engagement without explaining concrete mechanisms.

criteria.title: Create Opportunities for Dialogue and Exchange
criteria.intent: Participation becomes meaningful when people can exchange experiences and perspectives.
criteria.elements:
Describe:
Discussions.
Peer learning.
Reflection sessions.
Stakeholder exchanges.
Networking opportunities.
criteria.example_strong: "The project will facilitate structured dialogue and exchange among participants, encouraging peer learning, mutual understanding, and collaborative problem-solving."
criteria.avoid: Avoid treating participation as one-way communication.

criteria.title: Involve Stakeholders Beyond the Direct Target Groups
criteria.intent: Evaluators appreciate broader participation ecosystems.
criteria.elements:
Explain how:
Stakeholders.
Community representatives.
Practitioners.
Decision-makers.
Relevant organisations.
will contribute to project activities and results.
criteria.example_weak: "Stakeholders will be informed about the project."
criteria.example_strong: "Relevant stakeholders will be involved throughout the project through consultations, validation activities, dissemination events, and feedback mechanisms."
criteria.avoid: Avoid limiting stakeholder involvement to receiving information.

criteria.title: Ensure Inclusive Participation
criteria.intent: Participation should be accessible and meaningful for different groups.
criteria.elements:
Explain how the project will:
Reduce participation barriers.
Encourage diverse voices.
Create inclusive opportunities for contribution.
Ensure accessibility.
criteria.example_strong: "The project will implement inclusive participation mechanisms to ensure that diverse perspectives are represented and that all participants have meaningful opportunities to contribute."
criteria.avoid: Avoid assuming participation happens automatically.

criteria.title: Use Feedback and Consultation Mechanisms
criteria.intent: Participation should influence project development.
criteria.elements:
Describe:
Surveys.
Consultations.
Focus groups.
Evaluation forms.
Feedback sessions.
Validation workshops.
criteria.example_strong: "Regular consultation and feedback mechanisms will ensure that participant perspectives continuously inform project implementation and improvement."
criteria.avoid: Avoid collecting feedback without explaining how it will be used.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Long-Term Engagement
criteria.intent: Evaluators appreciate engagement that continues beyond project activities.
criteria.elements:
Explain how participants will remain involved through:
Networks.
Communities of practice.
Follow-up initiatives.
Dissemination activities.
Sustainability actions.
criteria.example_strong: "The project will encourage continued engagement beyond the project lifetime through ongoing collaboration opportunities, networks, and dissemination activities."
criteria.avoid:
Avoid presenting participation as ending when activities end.

What are the specific objectives of this work package and how do they contribute to the general objectives of the project? (2000)

general_context:
This question relates primarily to the Quality of Project Design and Implementation criterion. The evaluator aims to understand the specific purpose of this work package and how it contributes to the overall coherence and success of the project.
In simple terms, the question can be understood as: “Why does this work package exist, and how does it support the achievement of the overall project objectives?”
It is important to avoid describing activities only. Instead, the focus should be on what this work package is intended to achieve and how these achievements contribute to the wider project goals.
A strong response should clearly explain:
what this work package aims to develop or improve
what capacities, competences, or systems it strengthens
how cooperation between partners is enhanced
how it contributes to the effective implementation of later project phases
how it supports long-term impact and sustainability
The evaluator should clearly understand that this work package is not an isolated component, but a key structural element that ensures the project progresses effectively towards its overall objectives.
At this stage of the evaluation, the reviewers already understand:
the project needs
the overall objectives
the expected results
Therefore, they are now assessing: What is the specific function of WP2 within this overall framework?
Typically, a work package of this type contributes to:
strengthening implementation capacity across partners
ensuring a shared understanding of methodologies and approaches
improving coordination and cooperation mechanisms
preparing partners for the effective delivery of subsequent project phases
aligning practices and expectations across organisations
A strong answer always demonstrates a clear connection between WP2 objectives and the overall project logic.

criteria.title: Clearly Define the Objectives of WP2
Connect WP2 Objectives to the Overall Project
Demonstrate European Added Value
criteria.avoid: What will be the main results of this work package? (2000)

general_context:
The evaluator is asking:
"After WP2 finishes, what will actually exist?"
Results are the tangible and intangible changes produced by the work package, not the activities themselves.
Many applicants mistakenly list:
workshops
meetings
training sessions
These are activities, not results.
The evaluator wants to know what those activities produce.
Typical activity results include:
improved competences
trained participants
validated methodologies
strengthened cooperation
increased implementation capacity
shared knowledge
higher quality project delivery
writing_guidance:
This question comes after the objectives because objectives explain what you want to achieve, while results explain what you actually produce.
The evaluator expects a clear connection between objectives and results.

What qualitative indicators will you use to measure the level of the achievement of the work package objectives and the quality of the results? (2000)

general_context:
The evaluator is asking:
"How will you know that the activity was successful, beyond simply counting participants?"
Qualitative indicators measure quality, not quantity.
Typical indicators include:
participant satisfaction
perceived learning
quality of discussions
confidence improvements
relevance of training
quality of cooperation
applicability of learning

criteria.title: Explain How They Will Be Measured

general_context:
Here the evaluator asks:
"Which measurable figures will demonstrate that WP2 has been successfully implemented?"
Quantitative indicators are numerical.
Examples:
number of participants
attendance rate
completion rate
number of organisations represented
learning assessments completed
training hours delivered

This question assesses whether the division of tasks within the Work Package is clear, logical and balanced, and whether all partner organisations have a meaningful and justified role.
In simple terms, the evaluator is asking:
“Does every partner have a real purpose in this Work Package, and is the work distributed according to a clear and logical structure?”
This is not a request to state that all partners participate equally. Equal participation is not the goal of Erasmus+ projects. Instead, evaluators expect a strategic distribution of responsibilities, where each organisation contributes according to its strengths and has a clearly defined role in the implementation process.
A strong answer demonstrates who leads, who coordinates specific tasks, who contributes expertise, who supports implementation, and how all partners work together to ensure high-quality delivery of the Work Package.
writing_guidance:
By this stage, evaluators already understand the objectives and activities of the Work Package. What they now need to see is how the partnership will function operationally and whether responsibilities are allocated in a coherent and realistic way.
A common weakness is presenting generic statements such as “all partners will contribute” or listing tasks without explaining why they are assigned to specific organisations. Another frequent issue is unclear distribution of roles, which may suggest weak coordination or overlapping responsibilities.
A strong response should clearly demonstrate that each organisation has a defined purpose within the Work Package and that responsibilities are distributed in a way that ensures efficiency, quality and accountability.
connects_to:
This question is directly connected to:
-Work Package implementation
-Partnership quality
-Project management
-Quality assurance
-Efficiency and coordination
A well-structured allocation of responsibilities increases confidence that the Work Package will be implemented effectively and that all partners are actively engaged throughout the process.

criteria.title: Clearly Allocate Responsibilities Within the Work Package
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to see a clear and structured division of tasks among partners. Each organisation should have a defined role that contributes to the implementation of the Work Package. The answer should explain who is responsible for leading specific tasks, who supports implementation, and how responsibilities are distributed to ensure clarity, efficiency and accountability throughout the Work Package.

criteria.title: Match Partner Expertise with Assigned Responsibilities
criteria.intent: The evaluator needs to understand why each partner is responsible for specific tasks and how their expertise justifies this allocation. The answer should demonstrate that responsibilities are not randomly assigned, but are based on each organisation’s experience, competences and capacity. This ensures that each task is implemented by the most suitable partner, increasing the overall quality and credibility of the Work Package.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Effective Collaboration Between Partners
criteria.intent:
The evaluator wants to see that the Work Package is not implemented in isolation by individual organisations, but through active cooperation between all partners. The answer should explain how partners support each other during implementation, share knowledge, coordinate their efforts and jointly contribute to the development of outputs and results. This demonstrates that the Work Package is the result of genuine transnational collaboration rather than separated national contributions.

Please explain how the grant amount attributed to this work package constitutes a cost-effective use of the budget. (5000)

general_context:
This question belongs to the Quality of Project Design and Implementation criterion in Erasmus+ KA220 Youth projects. It assesses whether the budget allocated to the Work Package is realistic, justified and proportionate to the activities, expected results and objectives.
In simple terms, the evaluator is asking:
"Why is the budget for this Work Package appropriate, and how does it represent good value for money?"
This is not a request to repeat the planned activities or list costs. Instead, applicants should explain why the requested grant is necessary, how it supports the implementation of the Work Package and how it will be used efficiently to achieve the expected results.
writing_guidance:
Evaluators already know the objectives, activities and expected results of the Work Package. They now want to understand whether the requested budget is justified and whether the planned resources are proportionate to what the project aims to achieve.
A common weakness is simply stating that the budget is sufficient or necessary without explaining the relationship between the planned investment, the quality of implementation and the expected impact.
connects_to:
This question is directly connected to:
Work Package activities
Expected results
Project management
Quality of implementation
Financial sustainability
A well-justified budget increases confidence that the project will achieve its objectives while ensuring efficient use of Erasmus+ funding.

criteria.title: Crtieria1: Justify the Budget Allocation
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to understand why the requested budget is appropriate for the planned activities and expected results. The answer should demonstrate that the allocated resources are proportionate to the scope of the Work Package, reflect the complexity of the planned tasks and are sufficient to ensure high-quality implementation without unnecessary expenditure.
criteria.example_weak: "The budget is sufficient for the activities."
criteria.example_strong: "The budget has been carefully allocated according to the planned activities, expected outputs and implementation requirements, ensuring that all resources directly contribute to achieving the objectives of the Work Package while maintaining efficient use of Erasmus+ funding."
criteria.avoid: Avoid simply stating that the budget is adequate without explaining why.

criteria.title: Crtieria2: Demonstrate Cost-Effectiveness and Value for Money
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants evidence that the Work Package will maximise results while making efficient use of the available resources. The answer should explain how careful planning, appropriate resource allocation and cooperation between partners will ensure that the expected outcomes are achieved in a financially responsible and sustainable manner, demonstrating clear value for money.
criteria.example_strong: "The planned resources have been selected to maximise the quality and impact of the Work Package while avoiding unnecessary costs, ensuring that the requested grant represents a cost-effective investment in achieving the project's objectives."
criteria.avoid:
Avoid making general claims about efficiency without linking them to the activities and expected results.
