Describe the content of the proposed activities. (2000)

general_context:
This question assesses whether the proposed activity is well designed, pedagogically coherent and appropriate for achieving the objectives of the Work Package and the project as a whole.
In simple terms, the evaluator is asking:
"What will participants actually do during this activity, and why are these activities appropriate for achieving the intended objectives?"
This is not simply a request to list the programme or timetable. Evaluators want to understand the educational content, methodology and structure of the activity, and how these elements contribute to the expected learning outcomes and project results.
A strong answer demonstrates that the activity has been carefully planned, follows a logical sequence and uses appropriate non-formal learning methods to engage participants actively. The description should show that each component of the activity has a clear purpose and contributes to achieving the objectives of the Work Package.
writing_guidance:
By this stage of the application, evaluators already know the objectives of the Work Package and the overall purpose of the activity. They now want to understand how the activity will be implemented in practice and whether its content is suitable for achieving the expected outcomes.
A common weakness is providing only a list of sessions or topics without explaining their educational purpose. Another frequent mistake is describing the daily agenda in excessive detail while failing to demonstrate the connection between the proposed content, the learning methodology and the project objectives.
A strong answer explains the main themes, learning methods and progression of the activity, demonstrating that participants will move through a coherent learning process that supports the development of the intended competences and contributes to the project's results.
connects_to:
This question is closely connected to:
Work Package objectives
Learning outcomes
Non-formal learning methodology
Expected results
Quality of implementation
Impact
A well-developed activity description demonstrates that the proposed methodology is appropriate for the target group and provides confidence that the planned objectives can be achieved effectively.

criteria.title: Describe the Educational Content and Methodology
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to understand what participants will do during the activity and why these methods have been selected.
criteria.elements: The answer should present a clear overview of the educational content of the activity, explaining the main themes, topics and learning methods that participants will experience. Rather than simply listing workshops or sessions, applicants should explain how the activity has been structured to create a coherent learning journey. The description should demonstrate that the selected methodologies—such as workshops, group work, simulations, role plays, practical exercises, peer learning or reflection sessions—are appropriate for the target group and support the development of the intended competences. Evaluators also expect to see that participants play an active role throughout the activity and that the learning process reflects the principles of non-formal education.
criteria.example_weak: "The activity will include workshops and discussions."
criteria.example_strong: "The activity combines interactive workshops, group work, simulations, practical exercises and reflection sessions, creating a structured learning process that encourages active participation, peer learning and the development of practical competences directly linked to the objectives of the Work Package."
criteria.avoid: Avoid simply listing activities or presenting a daily agenda without explaining their educational purpose or methodology.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Coherence Between the Activity and the Project Objectives
criteria.intent: Evaluators want to see that the proposed content is not isolated, but forms an integral part of the project's intervention logic.
criteria.elements: The answer should clearly explain how the educational content contributes to achieving the objectives of the Work Package and the wider project. It should demonstrate that each component of the activity has been selected because it develops the knowledge, skills and competences required for subsequent project activities and expected results. Applicants should also explain how the learning generated during the activity will be transferred into practice after the mobility, ensuring that the activity creates long-term value for participants, partner organisations and the overall project. The evaluator should be able to understand why this specific content has been chosen and how it supports the successful implementation of the project beyond the activity itself.
criteria.example_strong: "The content of the activity has been designed to progressively build the competences required for the successful implementation of the Work Package, ensuring that participants acquire the knowledge and practical experience needed to contribute to future project activities and the achievement of the overall project objectives."
criteria.avoid:
Avoid describing the activity as a standalone event without explaining how its content contributes to the wider Work Package and the project's long-term objectives.

Explain how this activity is going to help reach the WP objectives. (2000)

criteria.title: Link the Activity to the Work Package Objectives
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to understand the strategic role of the activity within the Work Package and why it is necessary for achieving the planned objectives.
criteria.elements: The answer should clearly explain how the learning activity contributes directly to one or more Work Package objectives and why this particular methodology has been chosen. It should demonstrate that the activity is not a standalone mobility, but a carefully planned educational intervention that enables participants to acquire the knowledge, competences and shared approaches required for the successful implementation of subsequent project activities. The connection between the learning process, the expected outputs and the objectives of the Work Package should be explicit throughout the answer.
criteria.example_weak: "The activity contributes to the objectives of the Work Package."
criteria.example_strong: "The activity has been specifically designed to support the achievement of the Work Package objectives by providing participants with the practical competences, common methodologies and collaborative experience required to implement the following project activities effectively and to contribute to the development of high-quality project results."
criteria.avoid: Avoid simply describing the programme of the activity without explaining why it is necessary for achieving the objectives of the Work Package.

criteria.title: Explain the Learning Impact and Contribution to the Project
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to understand how the learning generated during the activity will benefit both the participants and the project as a whole.
criteria.elements: The answer should explain the expected learning outcomes for participants and demonstrate how these outcomes will be applied after the mobility. It should show that the knowledge, skills and competences acquired during the activity will be transferred into future project tasks, shared within the partner organisations and used to improve youth work practices beyond the duration of the project. Evaluators also expect to see that the activity contributes not only to individual learning but also to organisational development, stronger cooperation between partners and the long-term sustainability of the project's results.
criteria.example_strong: "Through collaborative learning, practical exercises and the exchange of experiences, participants will strengthen the competences needed to implement the project successfully. The knowledge acquired during the activity will be applied throughout the following phases of the project, shared with colleagues and target groups within each partner organisation, and integrated into future youth work practices, ensuring that the benefits of the activity extend well beyond the mobility itself."
criteria.avoid:
Avoid presenting the activity as a one-off training event without explaining how the learning will continue to support the project and generate lasting impact.


Describe the expected results of the activities. (2000)

criteria.title: Describe the Learning and Project Results
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to understand what concrete results will be achieved through the implementation of the activity.
criteria.elements: The answer should explain the expected learning outcomes for participants, the organisational benefits for the partner organisations and any tangible outputs that will be produced. It should demonstrate how these results contribute directly to the objectives of the Work Package and support the implementation of subsequent project activities.
criteria.example_strong: "The activity will enable participants to develop specific competences, strengthen cooperation between partner organisations and contribute to the creation of project outputs that will support the achievement of the Work Package objectives."
criteria.avoid: Avoid describing the activity itself instead of its expected results or using vague statements that cannot be linked to the project's objectives.

criteria.title: Demonstrate the Contribution to the Overall Project
criteria.intent: Evaluators want to understand how the results of the activity create value beyond the activity itself.
criteria.elements: The answer should explain how the results feed into later project phases, strengthen the quality of implementation, support dissemination and increase the long-term impact and sustainability of the project. It should show that the activity is part of a coherent project logic rather than an isolated event.
criteria.example_weak: "The activity will contribute to the project."
criteria.example_strong: "The expected results will provide the knowledge, resources and collaborative experience necessary for the successful implementation of subsequent activities, while also strengthening the long-term impact and sustainability of the project's outcomes."
criteria.avoid:
Avoid presenting the activity as an end in itself without explaining how its results support the wider project.

Expected number and profile of participants. (2000)

criteria.title: Justify the Number and Profile of Participants
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to understand why the proposed participants are appropriate for the activity and how their number supports effective implementation.
criteria.elements: The answer should justify both the number of participants and their profile, explaining how these are aligned with the objectives, methodology and expected results of the activity. It should also demonstrate that participants possess the motivation, experience or role needed to contribute effectively while benefiting from the learning opportunities provided.
criteria.example_weak: "The activity will involve youth workers from all partner organisations."
criteria.example_strong: "The number of participants has been carefully determined to ensure meaningful interaction, effective learning and active participation, while their profile has been selected according to the competences, responsibilities and target groups required to achieve the objectives of the activity."
criteria.avoid: Avoid simply listing participant categories or providing numbers without explaining why they are appropriate.

criteria.title: Explain the Added Value of the Participant Profile
criteria.intent: Evaluators want to understand how the selected participants contribute to the quality, impact and sustainability of the project.
criteria.elements: The answer should explain how participants will apply the knowledge acquired during the activity, share it within their organisations and contribute to the implementation of subsequent project activities. Where relevant, it should also explain how the participant profile supports inclusion, diversity and the transfer of results to wider target groups.
criteria.example_strong: "The selected participants will act as multipliers within their organisations, applying the competences acquired during the activity, supporting the implementation of future project tasks and sharing the learning outcomes with colleagues and wider target groups."
criteria.avoid:
Avoid presenting participants as passive beneficiaries without explaining their role in achieving the project's objectives and long-term impact.



Impact
How are you going to assess if the project objectives have been achieved? (3000)

general_context:
This question belongs primarily to the Quality of Project Design and Implementation criterion, although it is also closely linked to Impact.
The evaluator wants to understand how the consortium will verify that the project has successfully achieved its objectives. In simple terms, the evaluator is asking:
"How will you know that your project has been successful?"
This question is not asking whether you will evaluate the project. It assumes that evaluation will take place. Instead, it asks how the evaluation will be carried out, what evidence will be collected, and how that evidence will demonstrate that the project objectives have been achieved.
Many beginners make the mistake of answering only:
"Participants will complete questionnaires."
"We will distribute evaluation forms."
"We will collect feedback."
While these methods are useful, they only explain how information is collected, not how the achievement of the objectives will actually be assessed.
A strong answer explains:
what will be measured;
how progress will be monitored throughout the project;
which indicators will be used;
how evidence will be analysed;
how evaluation results will be used to improve implementation if necessary.
The evaluator wants confidence that the consortium has a structured evaluation system capable of demonstrating real project impact rather than simply reporting that activities took place.

A strong evaluation framework supports continuous improvement throughout the project and provides credible evidence that the Erasmus+ investment has generated meaningful results.

criteria.title: Develop a Structured Evaluation Framework
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to see that evaluation is planned, systematic and carried out throughout the project rather than only at its conclusion.
criteria.example_weak: "We will evaluate the project at the end."
criteria.example_strong: "The consortium will implement a continuous evaluation framework combining regular monitoring, interim reviews, partner meetings, and a final impact assessment to measure progress towards each project objective throughout the project lifecycle."
criteria.avoid: Avoid describing evaluation as a single final questionnaire.

criteria.title: Use Appropriate Indicators and Evidence
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants objective evidence demonstrating whether the objectives have been achieved.
criteria.example_strong: "Achievement of the objectives will be assessed through a combination of qualitative and quantitative indicators, participant evaluations, completion of planned outputs, stakeholder feedback, and monitoring data collected throughout the implementation period."
criteria.avoid: Avoid relying on a single evaluation tool or indicator.

criteria.title: Measure Achievement Against the Original Objectives
criteria.intent: Evaluation should demonstrate whether the objectives defined at the beginning of the project have actually been fulfilled.
criteria.elements: Explain that each objective will be linked to specific success criteria and that progress will be measured against the expected results established during project planning.
criteria.example_strong: "Each project objective will be associated with predefined success indicators, allowing the consortium to compare expected and achieved results and identify any areas requiring corrective action during implementation."
criteria.avoid: Avoid evaluating activities without linking them back to the original project objectives.

criteria.title: Use Evaluation Results for Continuous Improvement and Future Impact
criteria.intent: Evaluators appreciate projects that treat evaluation as a management tool rather than simply a reporting requirement.
criteria.example_strong: "Evaluation findings will be regularly reviewed by the consortium to identify strengths, address emerging challenges, improve project implementation, and generate recommendations that will support both the sustainability of the project and the development of future Erasmus+ initiatives."
criteria.avoid:
Avoid presenting evaluation solely as a final reporting exercise with no influence on project implementation.

Explain how you will ensure the sustainability of the project: How will the participation in this project contribute to the development of the involved organisations in the long-term? Do you plan to continue using the project results or implement some of the activities after the project's end? (3000)

general_context:
This question belongs primarily to the Impact criterion and is one of the most important questions in the Erasmus+ application.
The evaluator wants to understand what will happen after the EU funding finishes. In simple terms, the evaluator is asking:
"Will this project create lasting value, or will everything stop when the grant ends?"
Erasmus+ does not fund projects simply to organise activities during the project lifetime. The programme aims to create long-term change within organisations, among participants, and within wider communities.
Many beginners answer this question by saying:
"The website will remain online."
"The results will continue to be available."
"Partners will continue cooperating."
Although these are positive elements, they are not sufficient on their own. Sustainability is much broader than simply keeping project materials accessible.
A strong answer demonstrates that:
organisations will continue using the project results;
the knowledge and competences acquired will become part of everyday practice;
project methodologies will be integrated into future activities;
partnerships will continue beyond the funded period;
project results will reach new beneficiaries after the project has ended.
The evaluator wants evidence that the Erasmus+ investment will continue generating benefits long after the project is completed.

Sustainability demonstrates that the project's benefits will extend beyond the funded period and continue contributing to the objectives of Erasmus+.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Long-Term Organisational Development
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to see that participating organisations will become stronger as a result of the project.
criteria.example_weak: "The organisations will benefit from the project."
criteria.example_strong: "Participation in the project will strengthen the long-term capacity of the partner organisations by enhancing staff competences, introducing innovative methodologies, improving international cooperation, and integrating the project's approaches into their regular activities and future initiatives."
criteria.avoid: Avoid describing sustainability only in terms of maintaining communication between partners.

criteria.title: Explain How the Project Results Will Continue to Be Used
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants assurance that the outputs produced with Erasmus+ funding will remain useful after the project ends.
criteria.example_strong: "The project results will become part of the participating organisations' regular educational activities, with partners continuing to use, adapt, update, and promote the developed resources in future training programmes and initiatives."
criteria.avoid: Avoid presenting online availability alone as evidence of sustainability.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Long-Term Impact Beyond the Partnership
criteria.intent: Evaluators want to see that the project's influence will extend beyond the organisations directly involved.
criteria.example_weak: "The project will continue to be promoted."
criteria.example_strong: "Through continued dissemination, stakeholder engagement, and the open availability of project resources, the consortium will enable additional organisations and target groups to adopt and adapt the project's methodologies, extending its impact well beyond the original partnership."
criteria.avoid: Avoid limiting sustainability to the direct project participants.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Future Commitment and Continuation of Activities
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants confidence that the project will represent the beginning of a longer process rather than a one-off initiative.
criteria.example_strong: "The consortium intends to build on the achievements of the project by maintaining its collaborative network, integrating successful activities into regular organisational programmes, and developing new European initiatives that further expand the project's objectives and impact."
criteria.avoid:
Avoid making vague statements such as "we hope to continue working together" without explaining how this continuation will take place.


Please describe the potential wider impact of your project: Will the impact be equally spread among the involved organisations? What is the potential impact of the project on each participating organisation as a whole? Are there other groups or organisations at local, regional, national or European level that will benefit from your project? Please explain how. (3000)

Capacity Building
A convincing answer demonstrates that the project's impact will continue expanding through the organisations, their networks, and the wider educational, social, or professional communities.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Impact on the Participating Organisations
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to understand how participation will strengthen each organisation beyond the project's immediate activities.
criteria.example_strong: "Each participating organisation will strengthen its capacity through the acquisition of new competences, innovative methodologies, and enhanced international cooperation. Although the specific benefits will reflect each partner's role within the project, all organisations will improve their ability to deliver higher-quality educational activities and future European initiatives."
criteria.avoid: Avoid assuming that identical impact is necessary. Focus instead on meaningful and relevant impact for each organisation.

criteria.title: Explain the Wider Impact Beyond the Consortium
criteria.intent: Evaluators want to see that the project's benefits extend well beyond the direct partnership.
criteria.example_weak: "The project will be shared with other organisations."
criteria.example_strong: "Through dissemination activities, stakeholder engagement, and the open accessibility of the project results, the consortium will enable educational institutions, civil society organisations, public authorities, and other European stakeholders to adopt and adapt the project's methodologies, significantly extending its reach beyond the original partnership."
criteria.avoid: Avoid limiting the impact to project participants only.

criteria.title: Demonstrate Impact at Multiple Levels
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to see that impact occurs across different geographical and organisational levels.
criteria.example_strong: "At local level, the project will strengthen participating organisations and their communities. At regional and national level, the developed methodologies will be shared through stakeholder networks and professional events. At European level, the transnational partnership and openly available resources will facilitate the transfer of innovative practices across different educational and organisational contexts."
criteria.avoid: Avoid mentioning different levels without explaining how impact will actually occur at each one.

criteria.title: Explain the Multiplier Effect and Long-Term Benefits
criteria.intent: Evaluators appreciate projects that continue generating benefits after implementation and whose results are replicated by others.
criteria.example_strong: "The project is designed to generate a strong multiplier effect by enabling participants to transfer their knowledge within their organisations, encouraging stakeholders to adopt the developed methodologies, and supporting future initiatives that build upon the project's achievements, ensuring that its impact continues to expand well beyond the funded project period."
criteria.avoid:
Avoid presenting impact as a one-time outcome that concludes when the project finishes. Demonstrate how the project's influence will continue to spread through people, organisations, networks, and future initiatives.

Please describe your plans for sharing and promoting the project results: How do you intend to make the results of your project known within your partnership, in your local communities and in the wider public? Who are the main target groups you intend to share your results with? (3000)

Sustainability

Stakeholder Engagement
An effective dissemination strategy increases the visibility, uptake, transferability, and long-term value of the project's achievements.

criteria.title: Develop a Structured Dissemination Strategy
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to see that dissemination is planned throughout the project and not limited to the final months.
criteria.example_weak: "We will publish the project on social media."
criteria.example_strong: "The consortium will implement a structured dissemination strategy throughout the project lifecycle, combining regular communication activities with targeted promotion of project results to maximise visibility, stakeholder engagement, and long-term uptake."
criteria.avoid: Avoid presenting dissemination as a single final event or communication campaign.

criteria.title: Identify the Target Groups and Adapt Dissemination to Their Needs
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to know who the dissemination activities are designed for and why those audiences are relevant.
criteria.example_weak: "The project results will be shared with everyone."
criteria.example_strong: "The dissemination strategy will target different stakeholder groups using tailored communication approaches. Educational professionals, policy makers, civil society organisations, and the wider public will receive information through channels and formats appropriate to their interests and potential use of the project results."
criteria.avoid: Avoid treating all audiences as if they require the same communication approach.

criteria.title: Use a Variety of Dissemination Channels and Activities
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants evidence that the consortium will use multiple complementary methods to maximise the reach of the project.
criteria.example_weak: "We will use social media and a website."
criteria.example_strong: "The consortium will combine digital communication, stakeholder events, professional networks, and institutional communication channels to ensure that project results reach both specialised audiences and the wider public through complementary dissemination methods."
criteria.avoid: Avoid listing communication tools without explaining their purpose.

criteria.title: Demonstrate How Dissemination Supports Exploitation and Long-Term Impact
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants to see that dissemination is not only about visibility but also about encouraging others to adopt and use the project's results.
criteria.example_weak: "The project results will be promoted online."
criteria.example_strong: "Dissemination activities will actively encourage stakeholders to adopt, adapt, and integrate the project's results into their own educational and organisational practices, increasing the long-term impact and sustainability of the project's achievements across different sectors and European contexts."
criteria.avoid:
Avoid presenting dissemination solely as a communication exercise. Demonstrate how it will facilitate the practical use, transfer, and long-term exploitation of the project's results.

Project summary
Be concise and clear and mention at least the following elements: context/background of project; objectives of your project; number and profile of participants; description of activities; methodology to be used in carrying out the project; a short description of the results and impact envisaged and finally the potential longer-term benefits.
Objectives: What do you want to achieve by implementing the project? (500)

general_context:
This question assesses whether the project has clear and relevant objectives that respond to the identified needs and Erasmus+ priorities.
In simple terms, the evaluator is asking:
"What does your project aim to achieve?"
This is not a description of activities or results. Applicants should briefly explain the project's main objectives and the changes they want to create for the target groups.
writing_guidance: As part of the project summary, this is one of the first sections evaluators read. The objectives should provide a clear overview of the project's purpose and be consistent with the needs analysis and the rest of the application.

criteria.title: Present Clear Project Objectives
criteria.intent:
The evaluator wants a concise explanation of the project's main objectives. The answer should focus on what the project intends to achieve, ensuring that the objectives are realistic, relevant and aligned with the identified needs and Erasmus+ priorities.

Implementation: What activities are you going to implement? (500)

general_context:
This question assesses whether the planned activities are appropriate for achieving the project's objectives.
In simple terms, the evaluator is asking:
"What are the main activities of your project?"
Applicants should briefly summarise the key activities without providing detailed descriptions.
writing_guidance: This section gives evaluators a quick overview of how the project will be implemented. The activities should appear coherent, realistic and clearly linked to the project objectives.

criteria.title: Summarise the Main Activities
criteria.intent:
The evaluator wants a brief overview of the project's main activities. The answer should summarise the key actions and demonstrate that they form a logical pathway towards achieving the project objectives.

Results: What project results and other outcomes do you expect your project to have? (500)

general_context:
This question belongs primarily to the Impact criterion in Erasmus+ KA220 Youth projects. It assesses whether the project is expected to produce meaningful and lasting results.
In simple terms, the evaluator is asking:
"What will your project achieve?"
Applicants should briefly present the most important results, learning outcomes and benefits for participants, organisations and target groups.
writing_guidance: As part of the project summary, this section should give evaluators a quick understanding of the project's expected impact without repeating the activities.

criteria.title: Present the Main Expected Results
criteria.intent: The evaluator wants a concise summary of the project's main results and outcomes. The answer should explain the most important changes, outputs and benefits the project is expected to generate, while showing their contribution to the project's overall impact.
